Sea breeze and outflow induced storms were slow moving to the east with surface based CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Aloft at 500 mb temperatures were -8 to -10 degrees Celcius.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall produced 1.6 inches of rain in 30 minutes in West Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 49000 customers withouth power in the Talquin Electric COOP area, primarily in Wakulla County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down along I-10 at mile marker 245.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down near intersection of Mahan Dr. and Blairstone Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was north of the area with a high pressure ridge axis south of the region. Storms fired along the sea breezes and resultant outflows over a moist and unstable air mass with light steering flow in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood event developed across downtown Jacksonville when two storms collided over the area from the north and south. Around 740 pm, significant street flooding was reported in Riverside then by 755 pm significant flooding was reported in downtown Jacksonville. Areas near Hogans Creek experienced the most flooding. Jefferson Street was closed due to standing water 2 feet deep. At 805 pm, a resident of the Centennial Towers Apartments near Shands Hospital reported Hogans Creek had overflowed its banks and was approaching her apartment. Emergency Management officials reported that the maximum water height was 2 feet from the door of the first floor apartments.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough was north of the area with a high pressure ridge axis south of the region. Storms fired along the sea breezes and resultant outflows over a moist and unstable air mass with light steering flow in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and damaged a building near Oak and Goodwin Streets in the Riverside Area. The building did not catch on fire but it was significantly damaged. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface ridge was across north Florida while a trough was draped across south central Georgia. A mid level short wave trough traversed southeast Georgia during the afternoon over a very moist and unstable airmass. Steering motion was slow but primarily to the east. Numerous storms fired across the area during the afternoon and evening as the sea breezes and their outflows converged between Highway 301 and Interstate 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Palm Coast at Old Kings Road and Fanshawe Lane. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface ridge was across north Florida while a trough was draped across south central Georgia. A mid level short wave trough traversed southeast Georgia during the afternoon over a very moist and unstable airmass. Steering motion was slow but primarily to the east. Numerous storms fired across the area during the afternoon and evening as the sea breezes and their outflows converged between Highway 301 and Interstate 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 1.6 inches of rainfall from 4 pm to 5 pm. There was pooling of water and flooded ditches along Argyle Forest Blvd between Old Middleburg Road and Shindler Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level short wave trough traversed the area during the afternoon and evening which enhanced instability over the area. Sea breeze and outflow induced storms were slow moving to the east with surface based CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Aloft at 500 mb temperatures were -8 to -10 degrees Celcius.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2.75 inches was measured in Northern Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the area with a surface trough located north of the Altamaha River basin. This energy combined with a moist and unstable air mass enabled scattered severe storms to form over the area along and in advance of the sea breezes and resultant outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A possible funnel cloud was spotted at Fortune Parkway and McLaurin Road not far from the Avenues Mall. This was likely as a thunderstorm outflow converged with the east coast sea breeze in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the area with a surface trough located north of the Altamaha River basin. This energy combined with a moist and unstable air mass enabled scattered severe storms to form over the area along and in advance of the sea breezes and resultant outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported street flooding at the intersection of Gano Avenue and Debarry Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the area with a surface trough located north of the Altamaha River basin. This energy combined with a moist and unstable air mass enabled scattered severe storms to form over the area along and in advance of the sea breezes and resultant outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A structure fire was caused by a lightning strike on Red Oak Circle. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the area with a surface trough located north of the Altamaha River basin. This energy combined with a moist and unstable air mass enabled scattered severe storms to form over the area along and in advance of the sea breezes and resultant outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Highway 20 across the street from the Tackle Box store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the area with a surface trough located north of the Altamaha River basin. This energy combined with a moist and unstable air mass enabled scattered severe storms to form over the area along and in advance of the sea breezes and resultant outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A concrete light pole was blown down at the intersection of Highway 17 and Village Square Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface ridge was across north Florida while a trough was draped across south central Georgia. A mid level short wave trough traversed southeast Georgia during the afternoon over a very moist and unstable airmass. Steering motion was slow but primarily to the east. Numerous storms fired across the area during the afternoon and evening as the sea breezes and their outflows converged between Highway 301 and Interstate 95.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and blocking the road at Old Kings Road near Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate funnel clouds developed during the morning along isolated, but heavy sea-breeze induced showers. The first funnel cloud was sighted near Ft. Pierce and the second one occurred over Port Canaveral. Each funnel cloud was witnessed for 15-20 minutes and was reported by numerous individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter, a mariner and the general public observed and reported a funnel cloud occurring over the Banana River, just northwest of Port Canaveral. One or possibly two funnel clouds lasted a total of about 20 minutes and moved little during the period. The funnel was associated with an isolated heavy shower which developed along the east coast sea breeze boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across the central Florida interior. One of the storms became locally severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm became locally severe, with winds uprooting multiple large trees in Audubon Park within Orlando. Numerous power lines were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developing across the forecast area as an upper level impulse of energy traversed the region during the afternoon over an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down across Macclenny. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening surface front was near southeast Georgia with a surface ridge axis across central Florida. A mean 1000-700 mb ridge axis was over southeast Georgia with a light south to southwest steering flow over the local area. An unstable low level air mass was in place and combined with the sea breezes and outflows numerous severe storms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was struck by lightning on Chappie James Court. There was minor structural damage. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening surface front was near southeast Georgia with a surface ridge axis across central Florida. A mean 1000-700 mb ridge axis was over southeast Georgia with a light south to southwest steering flow over the local area. An unstable low level air mass was in place and combined with the sea breezes and outflows numerous severe storms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning stuck three homes in the same neighborhood. The strikes on each home were within minutes of each other. One home on Rolling River Blvd suffered major damage. The other two homes on Crooked River Road and Deep Springs Drive had minor damage. The cost of total damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening surface front was near southeast Georgia with a surface ridge axis across central Florida. A mean 1000-700 mb ridge axis was over southeast Georgia with a light south to southwest steering flow over the local area. An unstable low level air mass was in place and combined with the sea breezes and outflows numerous severe storms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind blew down 3 to 4 small trees about six inches in diameter on the White Oak Plantation property. Dime size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening surface front was near southeast Georgia with a surface ridge axis across central Florida. A mean 1000-700 mb ridge axis was over southeast Georgia with a light south to southwest steering flow over the local area. An unstable low level air mass was in place and combined with the sea breezes and outflows numerous severe storms developed across the area during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down across Amelia Island including Amelia City and Fernandina Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few discrete supercells and small lines of strong/severe thunderstorms developed across the region ahead of strong upper system and associated cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey crew, in conjunction with county emergency management, found the damage in this location to be consistent with an EF-0 tornado which touched down just southwest of Florida highway 267.  The weak tornado then tracked north-northeastward onto Holt lane. The damage southwest of Holt lane was limited to trees snapped or uprooted, with a few non-substantial structures damaged on Holt lane.  One outbuilding lost its roof. The tornado continued northeast crossing I-10 near exit 181.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The collision of the East Coast and Gulf Coast sea breeze boundaries over the central Florida interior resulted in several storms which produced damaging winds and penny sized hail from Oviedo to Orlando to St. Cloud. Trees fell onto two mobile homes and produced minor damage. A young boy in one of the mobile homes sustained minor injuries to his arm as a tree crashed onto the home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colliding sea breeze boundaries created a locally severe thunderstorm whose winds toppled a tree onto a mobile home in the Palm Valley Mobile Home Park in Oviedo. A young boy in the home experienced minor injuries to his arm as a result of the falling tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few discrete supercells and small lines of strong/severe thunderstorms developed across the region ahead of strong upper system and associated cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS surveyed the area around Lloyd, FL at I-10 near exit 215. The damage in this area was limited to a few trees and an awning to a gas station just off FL highway 59, and was deemed consistent with straight line winds estimated around 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few discrete supercells and small lines of strong/severe thunderstorms developed across the region ahead of strong upper system and associated cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down in the Cherry Lake area, along with power outages.  Wind speed unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small severe thunderstorm moved inland off the Gulf and into Dixie county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limb reported down on powerlines near 898th street and off Highway 346A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front moved across the area at the same time as a sheared mid and upper level vorticity maximum. Surface based instability was above 3000 J/kg and Lifted Index values were less than -7 deg C. There was sufficient CAPE in the hail growth zone as well as adequate shear. Severe storms developed across the Suwannee River Valley during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down. One tree broke the window of a mobile home. There were no injuries reported. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front moved across the area at the same time as a sheared mid and upper level vorticity maximum. Surface based instability was above 3000 J/kg and Lifted Index values were less than -7 deg C. There was sufficient CAPE in the hail growth zone as well as adequate shear. Severe storms developed across the Suwannee River Valley during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along 221st Road. A motorist drove into one tree, but no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable and moist airmass was over the local area under a very slow steering motion. Precipitable water was near 2 inches. When the sea breezes merged just west of the St. Johns River and east of Highway 301 in northeast Florida during the late afternoon and evening, numerous storms erupted. Heavy rainfall caused localized flooding, and one severe storm developed that produce quarter size hail over Putnam county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 4.25 inches of rainfall in 1 hour. The measurement was taken at the intersection of Blanding Blvd. and Aurora Blvd in Orange Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable and moist airmass was over the local area under a very slow steering motion. Precipitable water was near 2 inches. When the sea breezes merged just west of the St. Johns River and east of Highway 301 in northeast Florida during the late afternoon and evening, numerous storms erupted. Heavy rainfall caused localized flooding, and one severe storm developed that produce quarter size hail over Putnam county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hourly rainfall amount of 2.5 inches was measured in southeast Palm Coast between 10 pm and 11 pm. There was also some street flooding on the northbound lane of U.S. Highway 1 in Bunnell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore flow and shallow moisture spawned convergent bands of cumulus over the coastal waters. A funnel cloud was observed in one line near Crescent Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both emergency management and the public called in reports of a funnel cloud that extended a few hundred feet down from the base of a cumulus cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Igor produced long period swells that impact the northeast Florida coastline. These swells combined with moderate onshore flow produced high surf conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surf heights ranged from 4 to 6 feet at Jacksonville Beach. A strong north to south current was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Igor produced long period swells that impact the northeast Florida coastline. These swells combined with moderate onshore flow produced high surf conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Breakers of 5 to 7 feet were reported at Flagler Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Igor produced long period swells that impact the northeast Florida coastline. These swells combined with moderate onshore flow produced high surf conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Atlantic Beach Patrol reported 5 to 7 foot breakers with occasional 8 foot breakers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms produced high winds that damaged trees and roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph caused minor roof damage and also damaged a swing set.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms produced high winds that damaged trees and roofs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 65 mph blew a tree down on to a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture and strong easterly winds in place across the area allowed for numerous thunderstorms to develop with afternoon heating. The storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large swath of mature oak trees were blown down in a southeast to northwest direction along US Highway 92 between Moore Lake Road and North Forbes Road  east of Plant City. Some trees were blocking the road near Fristke Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was in place across the region, limiting moisture, but allowing for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms along seabreeze boundaries. Some thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and large tree limbs were blown down near the intersection of East Columbus Drive and North 32nd Street. A few trees and limbs fell onto cars and structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was in place across the region, limiting moisture, but allowing for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms along seabreeze boundaries. Some thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a wind gust of 67mph in Ybor City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was in place across the region, limiting moisture, but allowing for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms along seabreeze boundaries. Some thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A warehouse building near the intersection of East Adamo Drive and North 45th Street suffered heavy damage to an interior wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was in place across the region, limiting moisture, but allowing for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms along seabreeze boundaries. Some thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles and power lines were downed near the Crosstown Expressway and 50th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was in place across the region, limiting moisture, but allowing for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms along seabreeze boundaries. Some thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a viewer report of quarter sized hail near the Crosstown Expressway and 50th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture and strong easterly winds in place across the area allowed for numerous thunderstorms to develop with afternoon heating. The storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house and caused a small fire in the attic. Fire crews were able to quickly extinguish the fire by cutting a ventilation hole in the roof, minimizing damage to the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent short wave trough passed across northeast Florida during the morning. Widespread areas of heavy rainfall with embedded storms impacted the area in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a funnel cloud near the intersection of U.S. Highway 1 and Race Track Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The east coast and west coast sea breezes interacted with outflows from storms over southeast Georgia. These boundaries merged across the Suwannee River Valley of north Florida during the evening hours as a short wave trough was passing over the region in the mid levels. This feature increased vertical instability, and surface based instability was high was CAPEs over 2500 J/kg. A few severe storms erupted from Lake City to Gainesville which produced hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An RV Park near Interstate 10 reported several oak and pine trees were blown down. Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire broke out in the Cypress Knoll area of Palm Coast around 1:30 pm. Some homes had to be evacuated. The fire was contained by 4 pm by Palm Coast, Flagler County and Bunnell fire departments as well as the county's Fireflight Helicopter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 10 homes from 28 to 44 Esperanto Drive were evacuated due to a wildfire that broke out in the Cypress Knoll area of Palm Coast around 1:30 pm. Vehicles and personnel from surrounding fire departments contained the fire by 4 pm. There were no damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased surface dew points near 70 degrees due to onshore easterly flow increased daytime instability across the Suwannee River Valley of north Florida. During the evening a severe storm developed that produced large hail over Columbia county. The storm was produced along the sea breeze merger and other outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in the Deep Creek area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level short wave trough and associated vorticity maximum traversed a highly unstable low level airmass during the late afternoon. This energy enhanced storm development along the merging sea breeze fronts and outflow boundaries. A severe storm caused wind damage across interior northeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five trees were blown down along County Road 250 between the Columbia county line and County Road 229. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong storms developed over northeast Florida during the afternoon in advance of a cold front. Convection was enhanced by a deep upper level trough and its associated energy that was digging across the southeast states. Strong wind gusts were reported in the cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a mobile home's roof was partially torn off, located near County Roads 252 and 245. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong storms developed over northeast Florida during the afternoon in advance of a cold front. Convection was enhanced by a deep upper level trough and its associated energy that was digging across the southeast states. Strong wind gusts were reported in the cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was damaged by thunderstorms when debris was blown into its windows. The windows were broken. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back-door cold front moving from the northeast to the southwest across the forecast area and the afternoon sea breezes converged over a very unstable low level airmass during the afternoon and evening which resulted in a few severe storms across interior northeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail and gusty winds were reported by a local business in Lake Asbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back-door cold front moving from the northeast to the southwest across the forecast area and the afternoon sea breezes converged over a very unstable low level airmass during the afternoon and evening which resulted in a few severe storms across interior northeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down south of the Oklawaha River Country Store along County Road 301.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent short wave trough passed across northeast Florida during the morning. Widespread areas of heavy rainfall with embedded storms impacted the area in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.83 inches was measured in 30 minutes. Minor street flooding was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent short wave trough passed across northeast Florida during the morning. Widespread areas of heavy rainfall with embedded storms impacted the area in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county emergency operations center rain gauge measured 3.10 inches of rainfall since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back-door cold front moving from the northeast to the southwest across the forecast area and the afternoon sea breezes converged over a very unstable low level airmass during the afternoon and evening which resulted in a few severe storms across interior northeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Employees at the Handy Way food store estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over Mississippi and Alabama with a series of positive vorticity lobes rotating around it. One lobe traversed over the Suwannee River Valley during the evening which enhanced convection that developed along the sea breeze fronts and resultant outflows. One storm over Columbia county produced small hail around penny size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported penny size hail and some tree debris at the intersection of Interstate 75 and Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over Mississippi and Alabama. A light steering flow was in place over the local area. Deep moisture and an unstable airmass were in place. Afternoon and evening pulse severe storms developed along the sea breezes and outflow boundaries. The most intense storms were west of Highway 301.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged property along Shoreline Drive of Orange Lake in Northwest Marion County. Damage included a TV roof antenna that was bent off, a patio cover that was blown about 150 yards into the top of a neighbors tree about 30 feet above ground level, overhangs on the house were destroyed, tree limbs were torn off, and tops of trees were twisted. There was roof damage as well. The time of the event was based on radar. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was over Mississippi and Alabama. A light steering flow was in place over the local area. Deep moisture and an unstable airmass were in place. Afternoon and evening pulse severe storms developed along the sea breezes and outflow boundaries. The most intense storms were west of Highway 301.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oaks and Pines RV Resort reported large tree limbs were broken and feel down due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable and moist airmass was over the local area under a very slow steering motion. Precipitable water was near 2 inches. When the sea breezes merged just west of the St. Johns River and east of Highway 301 in northeast Florida during the late afternoon and evening, numerous storms erupted. Heavy rainfall caused localized flooding, and one severe storm developed that produce quarter size hail over Putnam county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A store clerk along U.S. Highway 17 southwest of San Mateo reported quarter size hail. The hail lasted for a short duration. Numerous power outages were reported in the area. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High swell from distant Hurricane Earl combined with onshore flow produced breaker heights of 7 to 10 feet along the northeast Florida coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Breakers of 5 to 8 feet were reported along Flagler Beach. Heights as high as 10 feet were occasionally observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High swell from distant Hurricane Earl combined with onshore flow produced breaker heights of 7 to 10 feet along the northeast Florida coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Breakers of 4 to 6 feet were observed with occasional 8 foot breakers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe storms develop near the east coast sea breeze front during the afternoon in advance of an approaching surface front from the northwest. Convection was enhanced by a passing upper level trough. CAPEs neared 3000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down across Jacksonville Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore flow and shallow moisture produced several convergent bands of cumulus clouds over the near shore coastal waters. One land produced a narrow waterspout, undetectable by NWS Dopper Radar. The waterspout was first observed east of Neptune Beach, then it was observed well south of Mayport as a funnel cloud about 5 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported 10 miles south of Mayport by a military forecaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough moved across South Florida from east to west during the day, focusing showers and thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Coral Springs around midday. No injuries were reported from smoke inside the home. Damage is unknown, but likely was minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Swells of 2 to 3 feet from a distant storm in the north Atlantic created ideal rip current conditions along the Palm Beach County coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 46-year-old Canadian businessman, in town for a conference, drowned at the Ritz-Carlton Hotel beach in Manalapan during an overnight swim. Seas were rough, with rip currents likely occurring along with swells of up to 3 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture and strong cold air advection on the west side of a 1000 mb surface low offshore of the Georgia Atlantic Coast brought a wintry mix of snow flurries and sleet during the mid morning hours to parts of Northeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow flurries and sleet were reported about 3 miles south of Worthington Springs around 8:30 am. Between 9 am and noon, intermittent snow flurries were observed along Northwest 39th Avenue in Gainesville by a Storm Spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface ridge axis was across south Florida with broad troughing over south Georgia. A short wave trough was moving east over the Gulf Coast region with a swath of drier air over south Georgia. In addition, temperatures aloft decreased which assisted in destabilization. A moist airmass and slow storm motion produced areas of heavy rainfall across north Florida in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter on Summer Breeze Drive in north Jacksonville measured 2.25 inches of rainfall in 1.5 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore flow with near shore winds 15 to 20 knots combined with buildings wells of 3-5 ft with periods of 11 to 15 seconds from distant tropical cyclones produced dangerous rip currents along the north Florida coast over the weekend. Many rip current rescues were done.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jacksonville Beach Life Guard station performed a total of 6 rip current rescues over the weekend. One near drowning occurred around 8:30 am in the morning at Jacksonville Beach before the life guards were on duty. The woman was rescued by Jacksonville Beach Fire Rescue then taken to the hospital in critical condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary thunderstorms across Vaca Key produced rainfall totals generally from 5 to 8 inches across Marathon and Grassy Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 8.28 inches was recorded at Marathon Airport.  Widespread street flooding occurred with water over one foot deep at the Turtle Hospital in Marathon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly unstable airmass combined with forcing from the sea breeze fronts, outflow boundaries and a passing short wave trough over north Florida triggered a few strong storms in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad surface and surface trough were positioned across south Georgia.  The trough extended east of the surface low toward the Atlantic Coast. A prevailing southwest flow regime was in place over a very unstable atmosphere. Afternoon high temperatures rose into the mid to upper 90s with dew points in the lower 80s. Convection first initiated along both the east and west coast sea  breeze fronts. A strong outflow boundary raced southward from convection that was over Duval county...and this feature triggered numerous strong to severe storms within a multicell line across north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered reports of blown down trees occurred across the Gainesville city limits between 430 pm and 6 pm local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed with a lightning strike hitting Leon County High School in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a fire on the roof of Leon High School. Approximately 300 people were inside attending a banquet, but nobody was injured.  Damages are estimated at approximately $15,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed with lightning striking a home in Grayton Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home along Garfield Street in Grayton Beach was struck by lightning, which caused a small fire in the attic above a rear bedroom.  Firefighters were able to extinguish the fire quickly and keep damage to a relative minimum.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved ashore as a weak tornado in Key Largo.  Very minor damage occurred near the immediate shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A retired air traffic controller observed a waterspout moving onshore from the Atlantic waters in the Winston Waterways section of Key Largo.  A few tree branches were downed with some patio furniture tossed about.  A minor power outage occurred nearby to 5 customers.  The duration was only a few seconds with path length around 400 feet.  Maximum winds were estimated between 50 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level short wave trough helped destabilize an unstable airmass and triggered a few severe storms during the afternoon along the sea breezes and outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down near Bulow Plantation Ruins Historic State Park. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad surface and surface trough were positioned across south Georgia.  The trough extended east of the surface low toward the Atlantic Coast. A prevailing southwest flow regime was in place over a very unstable atmosphere. Afternoon high temperatures rose into the mid to upper 90s with dew points in the lower 80s. Convection first initiated along both the east and west coast sea  breeze fronts. A strong outflow boundary raced southward from convection that was over Duval county...and this feature triggered numerous strong to severe storms within a multicell line across north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Southwest 82nd Way. The event time was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad surface and surface trough were positioned across south Georgia.  The trough extended east of the surface low toward the Atlantic Coast. A prevailing southwest flow regime was in place over a very unstable atmosphere. Afternoon high temperatures rose into the mid to upper 90s with dew points in the lower 80s. Convection first initiated along both the east and west coast sea  breeze fronts. A strong outflow boundary raced southward from convection that was over Duval county...and this feature triggered numerous strong to severe storms within a multicell line across north Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Millhopper Road. The event time was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms moved across the Florida peninsula under westerly flow over a moist airmass. Outflows converged with the east coast sea breeze to produce periods of heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was well south of the forecast area with an arctic airmass funneling over the area. Surface temperatures were in the 30s. Mid level moisture and a mid level trough combined to support scattered sleet and snow flurries across the area during the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and light snow dusted grassy areas and cars from San Mateo to Crescent City and Bostwick. The EMA stated it sleeted for 30 minutes in Crescent City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was well south of the forecast area with an arctic airmass funneling over the area. Surface temperatures were in the 30s. Mid level moisture and a mid level trough combined to support scattered sleet and snow flurries across the area during the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ocala Police Department reported light sleet around 5:49 am. Snow flurries were reported in Reddick in Northern Marion County around 5:50 am and in Ocala around 6:06 am. By 6:15 am, a HAM Radio operator reported sleet on Interstate 75 about 4 miles southwest of Ocala with minimal collection on the road. Around 6:30 am, a sleet and snow mix covered a car about 5 miles east of Ocala. A accident occurred around 8:15 am due to an icy bridge on the Williams Road Overpass over I-75. No injuries were reported but the bridge was closed for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few afternoon storms developed along the east coast sea breeze front. One lightning strike caused minor damage at a home in Jacksonville.

Firefighters contained a small fire that originated in the home's attic. There were no injuries, and there was only minor damage. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge was centered west of the local area over the northern Gulf of Mexico. Precipitable water was 2.25 inches off of the 12Z JAX sounding. Storm initiated along the sea breezes and outflow boundaries during the afternoon when instability was very high with surface based CAPES of 3000-5000 J/kg. Scattered severe storms developed during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along County Road 231 near Northwest 192nd Avenue, north of Gainesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms along the Gulf coast dumped heavy amounts of rain with some flooding reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell between 12:00 am CDT and 3:30 am CDT with a COCORAHS observer measuring 5.10 during that time period.  He also reported that there were several times when the water had risen enough on his street to cover it from edge to edge. The local overflow drainage rose to approximately six inches over the top of the wall, which normally only occurs during extended periods of rainfall that last a day or more.  No known damage or injuries occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Depression 5 were in place across the northern Gulf of Mexico with a strong south to southwest wind flow in place across the area. Choppy seas of four to six feet caused large breaking waves along area beaches and increased the risk for rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A family was swimming and rafting on the north end of Anna Maria Island near Sycamore Avenue, just off of Gulf Drive. A 71-year-old woman and her brother, age unknown, were pulled well out to sea by a rip current and died trying to fight the current. The woman's 78-year-old husband and 54-year-old son attempted to rescue the victims and were hospitalized due to extreme fatigue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal line of severe storms re-developed over northeast Florida during the early morning hours. Sufficient surface based instability with temperatures in the lower 70s and dew points in the upper 60s combined with a very strong southerly low level jet of 50 knots helped support severe storm development across the area. Widespread wind gust reports of 40 to 48 mph were recorded with the line, along with minor structural and foliage damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the Pine Eden area of Marion Oaks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a funnel cloud with a loud roaring sound as it passed over his location on the south end of the Jennings State Forest. He could hear tree branches and trunks breaking.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the deep south while the mid and upper level low moved over the Mississippi River Valley.  A surface low developed over south Georgia in the afternoon, and a trailing squall line moved across the area in the afternoon and evening. Strong low level shear, including a 50 to 60 knot low level jet, combined with adequate instability to produce widespread severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 15 trees were uprooted or snapped off from County Road 315C to Colonial Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in December, strong cold advection behind a cold front brought temperatures well below freezing to all of east central Florida, for three consecutive nights. A hard freeze occurred across most areas of the mainland peninsula. Temperatures early on Dec 27 fell to the upper 20s to around 30 across the region, with 2 to 8 hours of below freezing temperatures at many locations. Even colder temperatures were experienced on Dec 28, with many locations reaching the lower to mid 20s and the duration of below freezing temperatures ranging from 3 to 11 hours at most locations. A third night of similarly cold readings occurred on Dec. 29. A rough estimate of damage to fruit and vegetable crops across east central Florida from the combined impacts of the Dec. 14-15 and Dec. 27-29 freezes totaled 19 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures on Dec. 27 reached as low as 31 degrees in Vero Beach, 29 degrees on Dec. 28, and 28 on Dec. 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold advection behind a cold front brought temperatures below freezing to all of east central Florida for two consecutive nights. A hard freeze occurred across most areas of the mainland peninsula, with a freeze even occurring across the barrier islands. Temperatures early on Dec 14 fell to the mid to upper 20s across the region, with 6 to 9 hours of below freezing temperatures at many locations and 1 to 3 hours below 27 degrees in several areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate east flow kept the seabreeze pinned along the coast, with thunderstorms developing along the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported in the KTPA and KPIE observations, as well as by numerous Skywarn Spotters and the public between 653 PM and 707 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Swells from Hurricane Earl off the mid-Atlantic coast affected the Palm Beach County waters, causing rough conditions at local inlets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A charter boat captain died from injuries suffered when he fell off his boat as he approached Jupiter Inlet in rough seas. Northeast swells of 2 to 3 feet were observed throughout the day, although seas were likely a bit higher near Jupiter Inlet where the accident took place. ||The boat captain was pulled from the water unconscious by lifeguards, and died 3 days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Florida experienced the coldest December on record in 2010 as numerous arctic cold fronts moved across the state.  The record cold was in response to the Arctic Oscillation, which is an atmospheric circulation pattern that favors cold weather in Florida when it is in the negative phase.  ||There were three large freeze events where cold Canadian high pressure built into the area behind a strong cold front. The first of these occurred on December 7th-8th, with the Nature Coast experiencing 9 to 11 hours of sub-freezing temperatures and up to 8 hours of temperatures below 27 degrees. The most damaging of these systems occurred on December 14th-15th, with much of the area experiencing hard freeze conditions for 7 to 12 hours across the Nature Coast and 3 to 5 hours elsewhere. Sub-freezing conditions were recorded for more than 12 hours as far south as Sarasota County. The final freeze event occurred over a two night period on December 27th-28th, and December 28th-29th. Both nights saw sub-freezing temperatures for 10 to 14 hours across the northern half of the area and 4 to 8 hours across the southern half. In addition, hard freeze conditions were in place across the Nature Coast for up to 11 hours. ||Vegetables were the crops hardest hit by the freezes, with snap beans, cabbage, sweet corn, cucumbers, eggplant, bell peppers, and squash suffering the greatest loss. Aquaculture also suffered great losses in Tropical Fish, with up to 70 percent of the industry suffering losses. These losses occurred mostly during the December 15th freeze, which will be the date focused on below.||Estimates of crop damage are listed below by county for the month of December. Preliminary commodity losses were computed using data from the Florida Department of Agriculture. This data included the measurable loss of shipments from average for each commodity. ||Levy County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 11 hours across much of the county. The Usher Tower cooperative station near Chiefland and the FAWN station in Bronson both experienced the coldest temperature across the county of 21 degrees. This was a new record low for Usher Tower. Levy County has 44,870 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $3.29 million in damages to crops. ||Citrus County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 11 hours across much of the county. The cooperative station near Inverness experienced the coldest temperature across the county of 20 degrees, which was a new record low for the station. Citrus County has 6,146 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $0.45 million in crop damages. ||Hernando County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 12 hours across much of the county. The FAWN station in Brooksville experienced the coldest temperature across the county of 17 degrees. Hernando County has 8,212 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $0.60 million in crop damages. ||Sumter County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 11 hours across much of the county. The Bushnell cooperative station experienced the coldest temperature across the county of 21 degrees, which was a new record low for the station. Sumter County has 16,222 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $1.19 million in crop damages. ||Pasco County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for around 10 hours across much of the county. The St. Leo cooperative station in Dade City experienced the coldest temperature across the county of 30 degrees, which tied the record low temperature. Pasco County has 20,673 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $1.52 million in crop damages. ||Hillsborough County recorded sub-freezing temperatures for 8 to 12 hours across much of the county. The FAWN station near Dover experienced the coldest temperature across the county of 26 degrees. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of three intrusions of modified arctic air into South Florida occurred from December 6th through the 9th with the coldest mornings occurring on the 7th and 8th. Record low temperatures and the earliest freeze dates were rivaled across the area with temperatures for early December the coldest since 1937. This set-up the area for the coldest December on record and for one of the coldest months in recent memory. Significant crop damage was also reported during this event. A total of crop damage for the three December freeze events will be included in the event narratives for the late December freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across inland sections of Collier County fell into the low to mid 30s with the coldest temperatures reported across far north and east sections. The Florida Panther National Wildlife Refuge reported a low of 31 on the 7th and 30 on the morning of the 8th, and Immokalee and Golden Gate Estates hit 31 on the 8th. Freeze and frost damage was reported on tomatoes, cucumbers, and squash.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light freeze was reported across inland sections of Broward and Miami-Dade counties with isolated areas hitting the 32 mark during the morning of the 8th, however most locations remained in the mid 30s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of three intrusions of modified arctic air into South Florida during December occurred from the 13th through the 16th with the coldest mornings occurring on the 14th and 15th. Average temperatures during the period of 20 degrees or more below normal occurred along with a widespread freeze. This event was the coldest for mid December in nearly 50 years, colder than the early December event, and significant crop damage occurred once again with citrus, snap beans, sweet corn, squash, tomatoes, peppers, radishes, strawberries, watercress, sugar cane, and zucchini being affected.||A total of crop damage for the three December freeze events will be included in the event narratives for the late December freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low to Mid 30s occurred across metro and coastal sections of Palm Beach County during the mornings of the 14th and the 15th. The lowest temperatures ranged from 30 in Palm Beach Gardens, 31 in Juno Beach, 33 at Boynton Beach, 34 at Jupiter Inlet, and 35 at Palm Beach International Airport.

The third intrusion of modified arctic air into South Florida occurred from December 26th through the 29th with the coldest mornings occurring on the 27th and 28th. The duration of the third event was slightly shorter than the previous event, however temperatures where about the same. This event also ensured that the average temperature for December would remain well below average keeping the area on pace to reach the coldest December in recorded history for the region. Across northern and interior sections of the CWA crops that were already suffering from the previous two events were practically finished off.||Crop damage amounts for the three December freezes are included in the event summaries for this episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hard freeze once again occurred across areas west of Lake Okeechobee as temperatures fell into the 20s during both mornings of the 27th and 28th. Some of the coldest temperatures across the region during this period were: Palmdale 21, Brighton and Muse 24, La Belle 26, Ortona and Moore Haven at 27, Big Cypress 28, and Clewiston at 29. Heavy frost and freezing fog were also reported with this event. What cold sensitive crops were left in the area from the previous two events faced more severe damage with some crops being totally wiped out for the season. Some citrus crops were also frozen.||Crop damage amount estimates were unavailable for Glades County, although it is estimated that the county suffered little damage in comparison to neighboring counties due to the relative lack of vegetable and fruit crops in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across inland Palm Beach County dipped into upper 20s to low 30s during the morning of the 27th and 28th.  The temperature at Belle Glade hit 29 and in the Loxahatchee and at South Bay the temperature hit 32. It was estimated that eighty percent of the sweet corn was lost in the previous events across the county with more damage occurring during this event.||Crop damage amount estimate of $5.84 million is for all of Palm Beach County for the entire month of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of three intrusions of modified arctic air into South Florida occurred from December 6th through the 9th with the coldest mornings occurring on the 7th and 8th. Record low temperatures and the earliest freeze dates were rivaled across the area with temperatures for early December the coldest since 1937. This set-up the area for the coldest December on record and for one of the coldest months in recent memory. Significant crop damage was also reported during this event. A total of crop damage for the three December freeze events will be included in the event narratives for the late December freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across Glades and Hendry Counties tumbled into the mid 20s to low 30s with temperatures at Palmdale hitting 26 on both morning of the 7th and 8th. Brighton also fell to 26 on the morning of the 7th with Muse falling to 27 on the 7th, La Belle 30, Moore Haven and Ortona hit 31, and Clewiston hit 32. Widespread frost was reported along with crop damage to sensitive plants like cucumbers, corn, melons, beans, tomatoes, and eggplants. Citrus crops were expected to be OK.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of three intrusions of modified arctic air into South Florida during December occurred from the 13th through the 16th with the coldest mornings occurring on the 14th and 15th. Average temperatures during the period of 20 degrees or more below normal occurred along with a widespread freeze. This event was the coldest for mid December in nearly 50 years, colder than the early December event, and significant crop damage occurred once again with citrus, snap beans, sweet corn, squash, tomatoes, peppers, radishes, strawberries, watercress, sugar cane, and zucchini being affected.||A total of crop damage for the three December freeze events will be included in the event narratives for the late December freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low to Mid 30s occurred across metro and coastal sections of Broward County during the morningw of the 14th and 15th. The lowest temperatures ranged from 30 in Weston, 31 in Parkland, 32 in Deerfield Beach and Tamarac, 33 in Davie, 34 at Hollywood, and 37 at Fort Lauderdale/Hollywood International Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across inland sections of Miami-Dade and Broward Counties fell into the upper 20s to low 30s during the mornings of the 14th and 15th. Some of the lowest temperatures observed: 29 at the Miccosukee Service Plaza on Interstate 75, 30 at the Alligator Alley BSO, 31 near the Royal Palm Ranger Station, and 32 in the Redland, however growers reported temperatures down to 26 in the Homestead and Florida City area. The tomato, beans, squash, zucchini, and pepper crops took significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the deep south while the mid and upper level low moved over the Mississippi River Valley.  A surface low developed over south Georgia in the afternoon, and a trailing squall line moved across the area in the afternoon and evening. Strong low level shear, including a 50 to 60 knot low level jet, combined with adequate instability to produce widespread severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0  briefly touched down along County Road 121, and winds were estimated around 80 mph. There was significant damage to a mobile home. The cost of damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning storm produced localized severe thunderstorm wind damage in Orange County. ||A cell exhibiting rotation on radar tracked from rural western Brevard county, producing isolated severe thunderstorm wind damage in Viera from several miles west of I-95 to I-95 where a tractor trailer was tipped over. Just east of I-95, an EF0 tornado touched down and caused minor structural damage to several hundred homes, as well as considerable tree damage along a continuous path over 7 miles. Along this path, the tornado moved from the mainland, across the Indian River, Merritt Island, Banana River and finally across the beach side barrier island before moving into the Atlantic. See separate waterspout episode for over-water portion of tornadic waterspout track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down just east of Interstate 95 in the town of Viera in central Brevard County and remained on the ground for over 7 miles, then exited into the Atlantic. The tornado moved from the mainland, across the Indian River, to Merritt Island, then across the Banana River to the beach-side barrier island between Patrick Air Force Base and Cocoa Beach, then exited into the Atlantic. One hundred and forty three homes sustained minor damage in Viera, mainly damaged/destroyed screen pool enclosures, damage to soffits, gutters and some shingles. Minor damage to six homes occurred on Merritt Island and four apartment units and several vehicles in Cocoa Beach. Numerous trees were toppled or snapped along the entire swath of damage. One person was slightly injured in Viera due to flying debris/glass. EF assessment: DI2, DOD2, LB-EXP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed north of a warm front and was lifting across the Jacksonville area. A few stronger updrafts develop with embedded thunderstorms developing in the rainfall during the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a parking lot light at the River City Market Place in front of Best Buy. The concrete base of the light pole was blown out and the parking lot section in front of the store was completely out. No injuries were reported nor damage other than the direct damage to the parking lights was observed. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boat capsized during the morning due to large waves near the mouth of St. Lucie Inlet. Two men were rescued by another boat, but one of the men died before reaching shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent onshore flow generated seas of 6 to 8 feet over the near shore coastal waters. A 20 foot boat capsized around 800 am EST on January 24 near the mouth of St. Lucie Inlet. Two men were rescued by another boat, but one of the men died before reaching shore. The second man was treated and released from a local hospital. Neither man was wearing a life jacket.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought conditions which developed in October in Calhoun, Bay, Washington, and southern Walton counties came to an end on November 16th as rainfall increased.

A second and very strong arctic cold front moved through south Florida on January 9th. Very cold air of arctic origin in the wake of the front produced freezing temperatures and very low wind chills to all of south Florida. Freezing temperatures were noted over almost all of south Florida on the mornings of January 10th and 11th. Four consecutive nights of below freezing temperatures occurred over interior sections of south Florida from January 10th through January 13th. ||The combination of this episode and the first one between January 1 and January 7 produced the coldest 12-day period of temperatures on record at several south Florida locations. ||Crop damage was extensive and severe, with total damage estimates in excess of $500 million dollars area-wide. Thousands of customers experienced intermittent power outages during this period due to record-setting usage demands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 77-year-old man died as a result of hypothermia. He and his 93-year-old roommate were found unresponsive in their non-heated apartment in the Little Havana section of Miami on Monday January 11th. Both men were taken to the hospital, where the 77-year-old died the following day. The older man recovered from the episode. ||Wind chills dropped below 35 degrees and remained mostly in the 20s from the evening hours of January 9th through midday on January 10th. Temperatures dropped into the lower 30s during the early morning hours of January 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over south Florida along and north of a lifting warm-front. Several of the storms acquired supercell characteristics as they moved northeast into Martin and St. Lucie Counties. Several reports of wind damage and hail were received. Two individuals were injured by the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A park ranger observed penny sized hail occurring on Park Drive at the Railroad tracks within Johnathan Dickenson State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed in an unstable environment well ahead of a cold front. One of the storms became strong and produced small hail as it moved east out of the Ocala National Forest. The storm strengthened as it approached the coast, producing large hail on the barrier island east of central Daytona Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Daytona Beach citizen observed quarter size hail as the severe thunderstorm crossed the barrier island and moved to the Atlantic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions which began in the summer continued through the fall and into December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals continued to be below normal over most of South Florida in December, with most areas receiving only 25 to 50 percent of normal for the month. As a result, severe drought conditions (D2) continued across Glades County. Rainfall totals since June 1 were about 6 to 9 inches below normal in Glades County. Most wells across the area were running at about 10 percent of normal water levels. The level of Lake Okeechobee dropped to 12.4 feet by the end of December, which is 2.2 feet below normal. The Keetch-Byram Drought Index (KBDI) was in the 550 to 600 range, which reflects a high fire danger and very low soil moisture values.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions which began in the summer continued through the fall and into December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals continued to be below normal over most of South Florida in December, with most areas receiving only 25 to 50 percent of normal for the month. As a result, severe drought conditions (D2) developed over Hendry and western Palm Beach counties by the second week of December. Rainfall totals since June 1 were about 5 to 10 inches below normal in Hendry County as well as western sections of Palm Beach County. Most wells across the area were running at about 10 percent of normal water levels. The level of Lake Okeechobee dropped to 12.4 feet by the end of December, which is 2.2 feet below normal. The Keetch-Byram Drought Index (KBDI) was in the 550 to 600 range, which reflects a high fire danger and very low soil moisture values.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning storm produced localized severe thunderstorm wind damage in Orange County. ||A cell exhibiting rotation on radar tracked from rural western Brevard county, producing isolated severe thunderstorm wind damage in Viera from several miles west of I-95 to I-95 where a tractor trailer was tipped over. Just east of I-95, an EF0 tornado touched down and caused minor structural damage to several hundred homes, as well as considerable tree damage along a continuous path over 7 miles. Along this path, the tornado moved from the mainland, across the Indian River, Merritt Island, Banana River and finally across the beach side barrier island before moving into the Atlantic. See separate waterspout episode for over-water portion of tornadic waterspout track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tractor trailer traveling southbound on Interstate 95 in Viera (near mile marker 192.5) was tipped onto its side as strong thunderstorms winds pushed east across the interstate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and fast-moving squall-line ahead of a cold front produced damaging wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of January 20th, a warm front was moving slowly northeast off the Gulf of Mexico with a moist unstable air-mass moving slowly inland across the central Gulf Coast region. A series of slow moving thunderstorms repeatedly moved across the deeply saturated grounds of the western Florida Panhandle, producing numerous reports of flooding and flash flooding across the region. Prior to this event, northwest Florida was experiencing one of it's wettest winter season on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lambert Ridge Road near Oak Grove, Florida closed due to high water covering the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low tracked across the forecast area generally along the Interstate 10 corridor from the west. A strong pressure gradient brought gusty winds generally between 42 to 48 mph from 1:55 pm to 7:13 pm across portions of northeast Florida. Some tree damage occurred in Flagler county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across the road at County Road 80 and Catherine Street in St. Johns Park, which is located about 6 miles west of Bunnell. Another tree was blown down across the road at Martin Luther King Street and South Church Street in Bunnell. The cost of tree removal was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the north central Gulf. Heavy rain changed over to snow across portions of the central gulf coast as the low moved to the east. Snowfall accumulations ranged from a dusting to as much as 2 inches across the interior western Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Forest service reported 2 inches of snow in Munson and Berrydale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an already cold air mass in place, a cold front moved through the area on January 8th. An upper disturbance then passed over the area on January 9th, causing some areas from Tampa north to experience sleet and snow flurries mixed with the light rain. Very cold and very dry Arctic high pressure then built into the area causing temperatures to plummet into the teens and twenties across the area each morning from January 10th through January 12th, and remain below freezing for numerous hours each night. The morning of January 11th was the coldest of all the mornings. Numerous minimum temperature records were broken at several recording stations across the area each night. ||The extent of the cold combined with the multiple day duration caused significant damage to crops across the area. Marine life also suffered greatly, with the aquaculture industry devastated and wildlife enduring numerous fish kills and rescue efforts for manatees and sea turtles due to the Gulf of Mexico water temperatures dropping into the upper 40s. Nearly 200 of the roughly 5,000 manatee population perished from the cold. There were 47 sea turtles rescued from local beaches, 10 of which perished from the cold. Saltwater snook, bonefish, and tarpon endured widespread fish kills and caused the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission to extend the closed fishing seasons until September for snook and until April for bonefish and tarpon. Freshwater tilapia also suffered widespread kills, with some lakes reporting up to 95 percent of the population killed. ||Estimates of the crop damage are listed below by county. Preliminary commodity losses were computed using data from the Florida Department of Agriculture. This data included the measurable loss of shipments from average for each commodity which led to an estimated $249.5 million in damage in Florida due to the freeze events in January. The largest impact was on the tomato crop with an estimated 5.1 million cases lost. Other losses in order of greatest impact included tangerines, oranges, bell peppers, snap beans and strawberries. ||Levy county felt freezing temperatures for 12 to 14 hours across the county, with temperatures falling below 28 degrees for 8 to 10 hours. The Usher Tower cooperative station near Chiefland recorded the coldest minimum temperature across the county of 15 degrees, which broke the previous record low of 21 degrees set in 1982 and 1959. Levy county has 44,870 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $5.30 million in damages to crops.||Citrus county experienced freezing temperatures for 12 to 14 hours, with temperatures falling below 28 degrees for 6 to 8 hours. The cooperative station near Inverness recorded a low of 18 degrees, which broke the previous record low of 25 degrees set in 1959. The coldest temperature experienced across the county occurred at a site near Holder, with a low of 14 degrees. The average temperature for the month at the Inverness cooperative station was the fourth coldest since records began in 1948. Citrus county has 6,146 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $0.73 million in crop damages. ||Sumter county had below freezing temperatures for around 9 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for around 4 hours. The station near Bushnell recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 22 degrees, with the AWOS station at The Villages recording a low of 23 degrees. The county has 16,222 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $1.92 million in damages to crops.||Hernando county had below freezing temperatures for 10 to 15 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 5 to 6 hours. The Chin Hill cooperative station near Brooksville recorded a low of 23 degrees, which broke the previous record of 26 degrees set in 1942 and ended up being the sixth coldest month since records began in 1892. The ASOS station at the Hernando County Airport recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 14 degrees.  Hernando county has 8,212 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $0.97 million in crop damages. ||Pasco county had below freezing temperatures for 10 to 14 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 2 to 4 hours. The St. Leo cooperative station experienced a low of 27 degrees, with the lowest temperature across the county recorded at a mesonet station in north Dade City of 23 degrees. The average temperature for the month at the St. Leo cooperative station was the sixth coldest since records began in 1895. Pasco county has 20,673 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $2.44 million in crop damages. ||Pinellas county did not suffer damages to crops and had less than 100 acres of harvested farmland in 2007.  A 77-year-old man, his 72-year-old wife, and their 41-year-old daughter were hospitalized from being sickened by carbon monoxide after using a charcoal barbecue grill to heat their home for 12 hours due to the power going out from a blown transformer. The low temperature recorded at the Albert Whitted Airport was 35 degrees and the high temperature was 42 degrees, which set a new record low maximum temperature record. The previous record low maximum temperature was 48 degrees set in 1970.  ||Hillsborough county recorded an unofficial low temperature of 19 degrees from a home weather station in Valrico.  The low temperature recorded at the Tampa International Airport was 28 degrees, and the high was 43 degrees, setting a new record low maximum temperature. The previous record low maximum was 49 degrees set in 1970. The average temperature for the month at the ASOS station at the Tampa International Airport was the sixth coldest since records began in 1890. Hillsborough county has 44,661 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $5.28 million in crop damages. The Florida Aquaculture Industry suffered approximately $5.2 million in damages across the county. Excessive water pumping occurred at farms during this period to protect strawberries and other fruits, which caused a lowering of the water table in eastern Hillsborough county. The result was 751 complaints of dried-up residential wells and $2.6 million to stabilize 80 sinkholes from Seffner through Plant City. Major highways, Interstate 4, and several homes were damaged by the sink holes. More than 1,600 water distribution pipes throughout the city of Tampa suffered breaks. The damage varies from water mains to residential hookups and repair costs are estimated at $400,000. A minor leak formed in the Rowlett Park dam when the low water temperatures caused an epoxy seal on a concrete seam to contract and break. Costs to repair the leak are $15,000. Tampa Fire Rescue responded to several fires started by a space heater and  malfunctioning fireplace and home heating unit. ||Polk county had below freezing temperatures for 10 to 14 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 3 to 4 hours. The cooperative station at the Lakeland Linder Airport recorded a low of 23 degrees, breaking the previous low of 31 degrees set in 1977. The lowest temperature across the county of 16 degrees was recorded at a mesonet station in Kathleen. The average temperature for the month at the Plant City cooperative station was the sixth coldest since records began in 1892. The average temperature for the month at the Lakeland cooperative station was the eigth coldest since records began in 1915. The county has 115,721 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $13.67 million in crop damages. The Florida Aquaculture Industry suffered approximately $1.8 million in damages across the county.||Manatee county had below freezing temperatures for 5 to 7 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for around 3 hours. The ASOS station at the Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport recorded a low of 28 degrees, breaking the previous low of 29 degrees set in 1959. The lowest temperature across the county of 24 degrees was recorded at the cooperative station near Duette. The average temperature for the month at the ASOS station at the Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport was the fifth coldest since records began in 1948.  The county has 58,920 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $6.96 million in crop damages. The Florida Aquaculture Industry suffered approximately $0.9 million in damages across the county.||Hardee county had below freezing temperatures for around 12 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 4 to 6 hours. The FAWN station at Ona recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 25 degrees, with a station at Wauchula recording a low of 28 degrees. The county has 53,378 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $6.31 million in damages to crops.||Desoto county had below freezing temperatures for around 12 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 4 to 6 hours. The FAWN station at Arcadia recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 24 degrees. The county has 63,585 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $7.51 million in damages to crops.||Highlands county had below freezing temperatures for 7 to 9 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 4 to 6 hours. The cooperative station at Archbold recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 21 degrees, breaking the previous low of 22 degrees set in 1970. The average temperature for the month at the Avon Park cooperative station was the third coldest since records began in 1901. The county has 83,516 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $9.87 million in crop damages.||Sarasota county had below freezing temperatures for 5 to 7 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 1 to 2 hours. The FAWN station at North Port recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 27 degrees, with the C-MAN station at Venice recording a low of 31 degrees. The county has 5,969 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $0.71 million in damages to crops. The Florida Aquaculture Industry suffered approximately $0.9 million in damages across the county.||Charlotte county had below freezing temperatures for around 10 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 2 to 3 hours. The cooperative station at Punta Gorda recorded a low of 28 degrees, breaking the previous low of 30 degrees set in 1970. The lowest temperature across the county of 23 degrees was set at a station in Port Charlotte. The average temperature for the month at the ASOS station at the Charlotte County Airport was the third coldest since records began in 1965. The county has 21,663 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $2.56 million in crop damages.||Lee county had below freezing temperatures for 3 to 6 hours. The ASOS station at Fort Myers/Page Field recorded a low of 31 degrees, breaking the previous low of 32 degrees set in 1959. The lowest temperature across the county of 28 degrees was set at a station in Lehigh Acres. The average temperature for the month at the ASOS station at Fort Myers/Page Field was the tenth coldest since records began in 1902. The county has 18,308 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $2.16 million in crop damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lee county had below freezing temperatures for 3 to 6 hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area bringing severe thunderstorms with it. Large hail and wind damage occurred with the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck just outside of a home on Pilots Place in New Port Richey causing damage inside and outside the home. The bolt struck a palm tree on the side of the house, damaging several spots along the tree's trunk, leaving marks on the ground next to the tree, shattering a large potted plant, breaking up a portion of the sea wall, and rupturing a gas line. Inside, the skylight dome fell into the living room shattering a glass table top as it fell and the windows were blown out on that side of the house. No one was injured inside the house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were downed along County Road 314 in Fanning Springs.
