EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COMPS station at Port Richey measured a wind gust of 54 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Merging sea breeze and outflow boundaries within an unstable airmass contributed to scattered thunderstorm development. One of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds and large hail over the St. Cloud region of Osceola County during the mid afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree was downed due to severe thunderstorm winds near the intersection of 16th Street and Vermont Avenue in St. Cloud within Osceola County. The falling tree took out one or more power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A local man drowned after being caught within a rip current at Paradise Beach Park in Satellite Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents occurred along the east central Florida beaches, peaking several days earlier due primarily to a swell propagating into the waters. The swell and resultant rip currents were lessening by May 31, however there were many swimmers in the water due to the Memorial Day weekend holiday. A local man was pulled from the surf at Paradise Beach Park in Satellite Beach and was given CPR, but he did not survive. Life guards were flying rip current flags and 22 rip current rescues were made within Brevard County this day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Merging sea breeze and outflow boundaries within an unstable airmass contributed to scattered thunderstorm development. One of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds and large hail over the St. Cloud region of Osceola County during the mid afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail, quarter to half dollar size, fell across portions of St. Cloud as a boundary induced severe thunderstorm moved northward at 10 to 15 mph. The falling hail was video-taped and submitted to an Orlando TV station. The video clearly showed large hail on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area bringing severe thunderstorms with it. Large hail and wind damage occurred with the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were snapped, along with numerous branches broken off and several powerlines downed in Fort Meade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed within a moist environment due to interactions of numerous sea breeze and outflow boundaries. One early afternoon storm near the coast downed several large tree limbs. A collision of the east coast and west coast sea breeze boundaries across the central Florida interior resulted in several severe storms which produced large hail, up to golf ball size over Orange and Seminole Counties during mid evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Goldenrod.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A New Jersey tourist almost drowns in a rip current on Lake Worth Inlet due to onshore winds producing rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tourist from New Jersey was swimming just north of the Lake Worth Inlet at an unguarded beach when he was caught in a rip current. The current pushed him into the jetty where he sustained cuts and bruises, but the swimmer was caught at the end of the jetty were he was hoisted from the water and transported to St Mary���s Medical Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the metropolitan sections of Palm Beach County caused flooded streets. At the Palm Beach International Airport 7.4 inches of rainfall was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COCORAHS Observer 1.8 miles NNW of Green Acres reported 4.65 inches while the Palm Beach International Airport recorded 7.4 inches of rainfall during the event. Portions of Australian Ave, Banyan Boulevard, and Palm Beach Lakes Blvd were impassable during the morning of the 12th. ||No damage was noted from the flooding, and overall impacts were minimal due in part to the fact that the most significant flooding occurred during the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A New York University student on a spring break trip drowns in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A junior from New York University, plus friends, was on a spring break trip at Jupiter. The NYU student drown when he and a friend plus others got caught in a rip current. A Good Samaritan hopped out of a passing cab and dragged the friend to shore, but could not get to the student in time. The student was pronounced dead at Jupiter Medical Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the area and combined with a strong low-level jet and a strong upper-level jet in place over the state. The warm and moist environment in place allowed for numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop. A few tornadoes touched down and some areas received heavy rains causing flooding from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station 4 miles southwest of The Villages reported 6.19 inches of rain throughout the event causing minor street flooding. In addition, the cooperative station near Bushnell reported 3.68 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud developed along a line of showers associated with sea/lake breeze convergence. The funnel cloud was spotted about 3 miles east northeast of the city of Okeechobee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud developed along a line of low-topped showers in eastern Okeechobee County. These showers occurred along a sea and lake breeze convergence boundary on the northeast side of Lake Okeechobee. The funnel cloud lasted about 7 minutes, and was located around 3 miles east-northeast of the city of Okeechobee, Florida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance triggered a round of early morning thunderstorms over the central Florida interior. Several of the storms became strong and produced wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. One storm briefly became severe and resulted in wind damage within Vero Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm intensified to severe levels upon approach to the coast at Vero Beach. The storm produced winds which toppled a tree onto a house and caused damage to the roof. Nearby, the winds blew the arm of a construction crane into power lines, taking the lines down. Strong winds also blew a large tree limb down which took down power lines. The wind damage affected 16th street and the south side of Vero Beach south of the intersection of 4th Street and U.S. Highway 1. Damage reports were received from police officials and the Scripps Treasure Coast newspaper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the area and combined with a strong low-level jet and a strong upper-level jet in place over the state. The warm and moist environment in place allowed for numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop. A few tornadoes touched down and some areas received heavy rains causing flooding from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several road closures in Inverness, a few cars were stranded due to the high water near the intersection of South Apopka Avenue and US Highway 41, a wastewater lift station in Crystal River overflowed. Throughout the entire event, the Inverness Airport received 3.92 inches of rain. A mesonet site 5.3 miles north-northeast of Crystal River had 7.21 inches of rain. Other mesonet sites had 6.04 inches 3 miles northwest of Citrus Springs and 5.72 inches 1.6 miles north of Hernando. The cooperative station near Inverness received 5.56 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced hail in the Florida panhandle.

Numerous strong thunderstorms lifted northeast across east-central Florida from mid afternoon through early evening. Two of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds which downed several trees and large limbs within Orange and Volusia Counties. Many other storms produced winds below severe limits, resulting in downed small branches, small hail and funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was observed during a strong thunderstorm, near the intersection of Maguire Road and the Florida Turnpike in Ocoee.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed in association with a strong thunderstorm which developed just east of and shortly after passage of the east coast sea breeze boundary. The funnel cloud was observed near the intersection of Midway Road and the Torino Parkway, north of Port St. Lucie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell developed over the Everglades and moved into the Broward County Metropolitan Area where it spawned a tornado in Oakland Park. Heavy rainfall also occurred as a squall line moved through the metro area after the tornado event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple supercells and a squall line moved through South Florida the morning of the 29th. Heavy Rainfall and poor drainage combined to cover and even close some streets in Deerfield Beach including portions of SE 2nd Street, SE 8th Avenue, SE 9th Street, and SW 34th Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an already cold air mass in place, a cold front moved through the area on January 8th. An upper disturbance then passed over the area on January 9th, causing some areas from Tampa north to experience sleet and snow flurries mixed with the light rain. Very cold and very dry Arctic high pressure then built into the area causing temperatures to plummet into the teens and twenties across the area each morning from January 10th through January 12th, and remain below freezing for numerous hours each night. The morning of January 11th was the coldest of all the mornings. Numerous minimum temperature records were broken at several recording stations across the area each night. ||The extent of the cold combined with the multiple day duration caused significant damage to crops across the area. Marine life also suffered greatly, with the aquaculture industry devastated and wildlife enduring numerous fish kills and rescue efforts for manatees and sea turtles due to the Gulf of Mexico water temperatures dropping into the upper 40s. Nearly 200 of the roughly 5,000 manatee population perished from the cold. There were 47 sea turtles rescued from local beaches, 10 of which perished from the cold. Saltwater snook, bonefish, and tarpon endured widespread fish kills and caused the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission to extend the closed fishing seasons until September for snook and until April for bonefish and tarpon. Freshwater tilapia also suffered widespread kills, with some lakes reporting up to 95 percent of the population killed. ||Estimates of the crop damage are listed below by county. Preliminary commodity losses were computed using data from the Florida Department of Agriculture. This data included the measurable loss of shipments from average for each commodity which led to an estimated $249.5 million in damage in Florida due to the freeze events in January. The largest impact was on the tomato crop with an estimated 5.1 million cases lost. Other losses in order of greatest impact included tangerines, oranges, bell peppers, snap beans and strawberries. ||Levy county felt freezing temperatures for 12 to 14 hours across the county, with temperatures falling below 28 degrees for 8 to 10 hours. The Usher Tower cooperative station near Chiefland recorded the coldest minimum temperature across the county of 15 degrees, which broke the previous record low of 21 degrees set in 1982 and 1959. Levy county has 44,870 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $5.30 million in damages to crops.||Citrus county experienced freezing temperatures for 12 to 14 hours, with temperatures falling below 28 degrees for 6 to 8 hours. The cooperative station near Inverness recorded a low of 18 degrees, which broke the previous record low of 25 degrees set in 1959. The coldest temperature experienced across the county occurred at a site near Holder, with a low of 14 degrees. The average temperature for the month at the Inverness cooperative station was the fourth coldest since records began in 1948. Citrus county has 6,146 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $0.73 million in crop damages. ||Sumter county had below freezing temperatures for around 9 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for around 4 hours. The station near Bushnell recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 22 degrees, with the AWOS station at The Villages recording a low of 23 degrees. The county has 16,222 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $1.92 million in damages to crops.||Hernando county had below freezing temperatures for 10 to 15 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 5 to 6 hours. The Chin Hill cooperative station near Brooksville recorded a low of 23 degrees, which broke the previous record of 26 degrees set in 1942 and ended up being the sixth coldest month since records began in 1892. The ASOS station at the Hernando County Airport recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 14 degrees.  Hernando county has 8,212 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $0.97 million in crop damages. ||Pasco county had below freezing temperatures for 10 to 14 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 2 to 4 hours. The St. Leo cooperative station experienced a low of 27 degrees, with the lowest temperature across the county recorded at a mesonet station in north Dade City of 23 degrees. The average temperature for the month at the St. Leo cooperative station was the sixth coldest since records began in 1895. Pasco county has 20,673 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $2.44 million in crop damages. ||Pinellas county did not suffer damages to crops and had less than 100 acres of harvested farmland in 2007.  A 77-year-old man, his 72-year-old wife, and their 41-year-old daughter were hospitalized from being sickened by carbon monoxide after using a charcoal barbecue grill to heat their home for 12 hours due to the power going out from a blown transformer. The low temperature recorded at the Albert Whitted Airport was 35 degrees and the high temperature was 42 degrees, which set a new record low maximum temperature record. The previous record low maximum temperature was 48 degrees set in 1970.  ||Hillsborough county recorded an unofficial low temperature of 19 degrees from a home weather station in Valrico.  The low temperature recorded at the Tampa International Airport was 28 degrees, and the high was 43 degrees, setting a new record low maximum temperature. The previous record low maximum was 49 degrees set in 1970. The average temperature for the month at the ASOS station at the Tampa International Airport was the sixth coldest since records began in 1890. Hillsborough county has 44,661 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $5.28 million in crop damages. The Florida Aquaculture Industry suffered approximately $5.2 million in damages across the county. Excessive water pumping occurred at farms during this period to protect strawberries and other fruits, which caused a lowering of the water table in eastern Hillsborough county. The result was 751 complaints of dried-up residential wells and $2.6 million to stabilize 80 sinkholes from Seffner through Plant City. Major highways, Interstate 4, and several homes were damaged by the sink holes. More than 1,600 water distribution pipes throughout the city of Tampa suffered breaks. The damage varies from water mains to residential hookups and repair costs are estimated at $400,000. A minor leak formed in the Rowlett Park dam when the low water temperatures caused an epoxy seal on a concrete seam to contract and break. Costs to repair the leak are $15,000. Tampa Fire Rescue responded to several fires started by a space heater and  malfunctioning fireplace and home heating unit. ||Polk county had below freezing temperatures for 10 to 14 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 3 to 4 hours. The cooperative station at the Lakeland Linder Airport recorded a low of 23 degrees, breaking the previous low of 31 degrees set in 1977. The lowest temperature across the county of 16 degrees was recorded at a mesonet station in Kathleen. The average temperature for the month at the Plant City cooperative station was the sixth coldest since records began in 1892. The average temperature for the month at the Lakeland cooperative station was the eigth coldest since records began in 1915. The county has 115,721 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $13.67 million in crop damages. The Florida Aquaculture Industry suffered approximately $1.8 million in damages across the county.||Manatee county had below freezing temperatures for 5 to 7 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for around 3 hours. The ASOS station at the Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport recorded a low of 28 degrees, breaking the previous low of 29 degrees set in 1959. The lowest temperature across the county of 24 degrees was recorded at the cooperative station near Duette. The average temperature for the month at the ASOS station at the Sarasota-Bradenton International Airport was the fifth coldest since records began in 1948.  The county has 58,920 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $6.96 million in crop damages. The Florida Aquaculture Industry suffered approximately $0.9 million in damages across the county.||Hardee county had below freezing temperatures for around 12 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 4 to 6 hours. The FAWN station at Ona recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 25 degrees, with a station at Wauchula recording a low of 28 degrees. The county has 53,378 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $6.31 million in damages to crops.||Desoto county had below freezing temperatures for around 12 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 4 to 6 hours. The FAWN station at Arcadia recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 24 degrees. The county has 63,585 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $7.51 million in damages to crops.||Highlands county had below freezing temperatures for 7 to 9 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 4 to 6 hours. The cooperative station at Archbold recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 21 degrees, breaking the previous low of 22 degrees set in 1970. The average temperature for the month at the Avon Park cooperative station was the third coldest since records began in 1901. The county has 83,516 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $9.87 million in crop damages.||Sarasota county had below freezing temperatures for 5 to 7 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 1 to 2 hours. The FAWN station at North Port recorded the lowest temperature across the county of 27 degrees, with the C-MAN station at Venice recording a low of 31 degrees. The county has 5,969 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $0.71 million in damages to crops. The Florida Aquaculture Industry suffered approximately $0.9 million in damages across the county.||Charlotte county had below freezing temperatures for around 10 hours, with temperatures below 28 degrees for 2 to 3 hours. The cooperative station at Punta Gorda recorded a low of 28 degrees, breaking the previous low of 30 degrees set in 1970. The lowest temperature across the county of 23 degrees was set at a station in Port Charlotte. The average temperature for the month at the ASOS station at the Charlotte County Airport was the third coldest since records began in 1965. The county has 21,663 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $2.56 million in crop damages.||Lee county had below freezing temperatures for 3 to 6 hours. The ASOS station at Fort Myers/Page Field recorded a low of 31 degrees, breaking the previous low of 32 degrees set in 1959. The lowest temperature across the county of 28 degrees was set at a station in Lehigh Acres. The average temperature for the month at the ASOS station at Fort Myers/Page Field was the tenth coldest since records began in 1902. The county has 18,308 acres of harvested farmland, which is approximately $2.16 million in crop damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Levy county felt freezing temperatures for 12 to 14 hours across the county, with temperatures falling below 28 degrees for 8 to 10 hours. The Usher Tower cooperative station near Chiefland recorded the coldest minimum temperature across the county of 15 degrees, which broke the previous record low of 21 degrees set in 1982 and 1959. The county has 44,870 acres of harvested farmland, which translates into approximately $5.30 million in damages to crops.

An upper disturbance then passed over the area on January 9th, causing some areas from Tampa north to experience sleet and snow flurries mixed with the light rain. Very cold and very dry Arctic high pressure then built into the area causing temperatures to plummet into the teens and twenties across the area each morning from January 10th through January 12th, and remain below freezing for numerous hours each night. The morning of January 11th was the coldest of all the mornings. Numerous minimum temperature records were broken at several recording stations across the area each night. ||The extent of the cold combined with the multiple day duration caused significant damage to crops across the area. Marine life also suffered greatly, with the aquaculture industry devastated and wildlife enduring numerous fish kills and rescue efforts for manatees and sea turtles due to the Gulf of Mexico water temperatures dropping into the upper 40s. Nearly 200 of the roughly 5,000 manatee population perished from the cold. There were 47 sea turtles rescued from local beaches, 10 of which perished from the cold. Saltwater snook, bonefish, and tarpon endured widespread fish kills and caused the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission to extend the closed fishing seasons until September for snook and until April for bonefish and tarpon. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an already cold air mass in place, a cold front moved through the area on January 8th. An upper disturbance then passed over the area on January 9th, causing some areas from Tampa north to experience sleet and snow flurries mixed with the light rain. Very cold and very dry Arctic high pressure then built into the area causing temperatures to plummet into the teens and twenties across the area each morning from January 10th through January 12th, and remain below freezing for numerous hours each night. The morning of January 11th was the coldest of all the mornings. Numerous minimum temperature records were broken at several recording stations across the area each night. ||The extent of the cold combined with the multiple day duration caused significant damage to crops across the area. Marine life also suffered greatly, with the aquaculture industry devastated and wildlife enduring numerous fish kills and rescue efforts for manatees and sea turtles due to the Gulf of Mexico water temperatures dropping into the upper 40s. Nearly 200 of the roughly 5,000 manatee population perished from the cold. There were 47 sea turtles rescued from local beaches, 10 of which perished from the cold. Saltwater snook, bonefish, and tarpon endured widespread fish kills and caused the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission to extend the closed fishing seasons until September for snook and until April for bonefish and tarpon. Freshwater tilapia also suffered widespread kills, with some lakes reporting up to 95 percent of the population killed. ||Estimates of the crop damage are listed below by county. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong cold fronts brought reinforcing arctic air masses into east central Florida over a consecutive 12-day period.  From January 2 through January 14, high temperatures did not exceed 60 degrees on nine to 12 days, with several days recording high temperatures only in the 40s. Several low maximum temperature records were set. During this period, low temperatures fell to or below 40 degrees for 12 consecutive mornings across nearly all of east central Florida. Six to nine mornings saw temperatures fall to 32 degrees or less.  Many areas experienced low temperatures in the mid to upper 20s, with a few isolated, rural spots falling to near 20 degrees. Several record low temperature records were set. Frost occurred during several mornings. Long durations of temperatures in the 20s damaged or killed a considerable amount of vegetation, with agricultural crops, citrus, and ornamental flowers. Agricultural reports estimate a third of the Florida Winter fruit and vegetable production were lost, causing hundreds of millions of dollars in losses (state-wide). Also, winds remained near 10 mph on several nights, producing wind chill values in the teens and lower 20s.||Following a cold front on January 9, some sleet, snow and freezing rain mixed with a band of light rain from Kissimmee to Palm Bay northward. A slight accumulation of mixed precipitation occurred on vehicles, pool screen enclosures and plants. See separate Storm Data entry for additional details concerning this winter weather event.||A very rough cost estimate for (direct weather-related) freeze losses to fruit, vegetable and citrus crops across east-central Florida during January totaled near 14.5 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures did not exceed 60 degrees during most days between January 2 and 13. Low temperatures fell to 40 degrees or less during most nights and 32 degrees or below on many of the nights. Minimum temperatures reached the mid to upper 20s during several mornings. Frost also occurred on many mornings. Long durations of freezing temperatures resulted in considerable crop damage. In addition, Winds near 10 mph occurred on several nights, producing wind chill temperatures in the teens and lower 20s.||A very rough cost estimate for (direct weather-related) freeze losses to fruit, vegetable and citrus crops across Okeechobee County during January totaled 1.68 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding over many areas across northwest FL and the adjacent panhandle.  Wakulla county reported 6-7 inches of rain during the mid to late morning, with 3-6 inches across most of the region beginning late Wednesday night (Jan. 20th). There were widespread reports of flooded roadways, with water in some homes in Wakulla county.  FSU campus in Tallahassee reported 50-60 cars flooded.  Two hikers were stranded and clinging to trees along the Florida Trail near Freeport in Walton county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures did not exceed 60 degrees for 11 of 12 days between January 2 and 13 at Orlando International Airport. Four days experienced high temperatures only in the 40s (high temperature of 40 degrees on January 9). Daily low maximum temperature records were tied or broken on five days. Low temperatures fell to 40 degrees or less for 12 consecutive days and 32 degrees or below on six days. Three days experienced minimum temperatures in the mid to upper 20s, with the coldest temperature of 27 degrees on January 12. Three daily minimum temperature records were tied or broken. Similar temperatures occurred across the remainder of Orange County, with even colder weather across northwest, rural portions of the county. Frost also occurred on many mornings. Long durations of freezing temperatures resulted in considerable crop damage. In addition, Winds near 10 mph occurred on several nights, producing wind chill temperatures in the teens and lower 20s.||A very rough cost estimate for (direct weather-related) freeze losses to fruit, vegetable and citrus crops across Orange County during January totaled 0.84 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow around high pressure, increasing atmospheric moisture, and a passing mid level short wave trough phased across our western Florida zones during the evening to yield a few severe storms that produced widespread wind damage and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported at 1402 SW Tommy Light Street in Fort White.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong cold fronts brought reinforcing arctic air masses into east central Florida over a consecutive 12-day period.  From January 2 through January 14, high temperatures did not exceed 60 degrees on nine to 12 days, with several days recording high temperatures only in the 40s. Several low maximum temperature records were set. During this period, low temperatures fell to or below 40 degrees for 12 consecutive mornings across nearly all of east central Florida. Six to nine mornings saw temperatures fall to 32 degrees or less.  Many areas experienced low temperatures in the mid to upper 20s, with a few isolated, rural spots falling to near 20 degrees. Several record low temperature records were set. Frost occurred during several mornings. Long durations of temperatures in the 20s damaged or killed a considerable amount of vegetation, with agricultural crops, citrus, and ornamental flowers. Agricultural reports estimate a third of the Florida Winter fruit and vegetable production were lost, causing hundreds of millions of dollars in losses (state-wide). Also, winds remained near 10 mph on several nights, producing wind chill values in the teens and lower 20s.||Following a cold front on January 9, some sleet, snow and freezing rain mixed with a band of light rain from Kissimmee to Palm Bay northward. A slight accumulation of mixed precipitation occurred on vehicles, pool screen enclosures and plants. See separate Storm Data entry for additional details concerning this winter weather event.||A very rough cost estimate for (direct weather-related) freeze losses to fruit, vegetable and citrus crops across east-central Florida during January totaled near 14.5 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures did not exceed 60 degrees for 12 consecutive days (January 2-13) at Daytona Beach. Four days experienced high temperatures only in the 40s (high temperature of 40 degrees on January 9). A daily low maximum temperature record was tied or broken on one day. Low temperatures fell to 40 degrees or less for 12 consecutive days and 32 degrees or below on nine days. Five days experienced minimum temperatures in the mid to upper 20s, with the coldest temperature of 27 degrees on January 10. Three daily minimum temperature records were tied or broken. Similar temperatures occurred across the remainder of eastern Volusia County, with even colder weather across interior, rural portions of the county. Frost also occurred on many mornings. Long durations of freezing temperatures resulted in considerable crop damage.||A very rough cost estimate for (direct weather-related) freeze losses to fruit, vegetable and citrus crops across Volusia County during January totaled 0.76 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Measurable snowfall (1-1.5) was observed over Walton/Holmes Co., with trace amounts observed over Washington, Jackson, Calhoun, Gadsen and Leon counties.  This occurred along the southern periphery of a more substantial snow band producing 4-7 inch maximum snowfall into southeastern AL and southwestern GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rare measurable snow over inland portions of Walton county. One inch was measured at De Funiak Springs water department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
Thunderstorms produced sporadic large hail and damaging winds, along with more widespread flooding through the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees reported down near Johnson Rd. and highway 221.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving squall line producing damaging winds and a tornado in Key Largo.  The tornado was observed as a waterspout in Florida bay just offshore, which made landfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout accompanying a line of severe thunderstorms moved onshore the Florida Bay side of Key Largo at the Marriott Florida Bay Resort.  A tree was blown down blocking part of U.S. Highway 1 in front of the hotel.  An outdoor day spa consisting of a thatched roof and bamboo shutters and interior mirrors was destroyed.  Weighted deck loungers were blown across the pool deck and significant landscaping damage occurred on the waterfront side of the hotel.  The tornado quickly dissipated over U.S. Highway 1, and was likely a non-supercell spawned waterspout.  Estimated maximum winds were near 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed within a moist environment due to interactions of numerous sea breeze and outflow boundaries. One early afternoon storm near the coast downed several large tree limbs. A collision of the east coast and west coast sea breeze boundaries across the central Florida interior resulted in several severe storms which produced large hail, up to golf ball size over Orange and Seminole Counties during mid evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operator relayed a spotter report of golf ball sized hail at highway 436 near Casselberry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system and associated cold front brought heavy rain and localized flooding to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total rainfall of 6.52 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system and associated cold front brought heavy rain and localized flooding to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station measured 3.97 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man was killed while attempting to put out a wildfire in the Woodhaven subdivision in Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was killed while trying to extinguish a wildfire in the Woodhaven subdivision in Orlando. The man had ignited a barrel fire in his backyard, and the barrel got knocked over onto the ground. The wildfire spread quickly as conditions were extremely dry across the Orlando area. The man became overwhelmed by the fire as it spread and it became too much for him to handle. Firefighters eventually arrived on the scene and extinguished the flames.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down along County road 207A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light southwest steering flow was over the area with scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms forming along the sea breezes and resultant outflows. A lone reported of a funnel cloud was received in Gilchrist county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near the intersection of State Road 47 and County Road 340 over a corn field. The report was originally relayed as a tornado, but after site inspection there was no damage noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe and produced damaging winds, affecting a small area in St. Cloud. Another strong storm west of Vero Beach produced an outflow boundary which intersected a newly developing cell, resulting in funnel cloud and brief tornado (landspout) touchdown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near the intersection of Highway 60 and Interstate 95, west of Vero Beach by several individuals, including a County Sheriff, pilot, Vero Beach Weather Observer and the public. A storm chaser video-taped a brief tornado touchdown in association with the funnel cloud. Radar imagery indicated that the tornado was a landspout which formed as an outflow boundary from strong convection farther west intersected a newly developed storm to the east of the main convection. The tornado touched down in a rural area and no damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Suwannee River State Park official reported several large tree limbs were blown down. The time of the event is based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were blown down along Kelly Smith Ranch Road off of West River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout developed over the St. Johns River north of Palatka and was spotted near Racy Point. The spout formed from a low level boundary collision between the east coast sea breeze and outflow from a severe storm moving northward from eastern Putnam County. There were sporadic reports of funnel clouds from U.S. Highway 17 then from State Road 207 around 5:55 pm however locations of the funnel cloud were given in various locations. There was no mesocyclone nor low level rotational signature on Doppler radar and environmental conditions were not favorable for mesocyclone tornadoes (i.e. no wind shear). There was no damage with the vortex and it never made landfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the area with a surface trough located north of the Altamaha River basin. This energy combined with a moist and unstable air mass enabled scattered severe storms to form over the area along and in advance of the sea breezes and resultant outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree was blown down across Pine Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across east central Florida as numerous small-scale boundaries interacted within a very hot and unstable atmosphere. As two isolated storms merged along the St. Johns River along the border of Seminole and Volusia Counties, a downburst likely produced winds which knocked over numerous trees. Also, as boundaries collided over central Orange County, a north to south line of strong storms developed and produced nickel sized hail. Lightning destroyed a large estate size home in Longwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A State Emergency Manager observed nickel size hail near the intersection of John Young Parkway and Orange Blossom Trail in Orlando during a strong thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across east central Florida as numerous small-scale boundaries interacted within a very hot and unstable atmosphere. As two isolated storms merged along the St. Johns River along the border of Seminole and Volusia Counties, a downburst likely produced winds which knocked over numerous trees. Also, as boundaries collided over central Orange County, a north to south line of strong storms developed and produced nickel sized hail. Lightning destroyed a large estate size home in Longwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two isolated thunderstorms merged over Geneva and likely produced severe, but localized downburst winds. A Division of Forestry official reported five oak trees toppled and uprooted adjacent to the St. Johns River in Geneva.

Also, as boundaries collided over central Orange County, a north to south line of strong storms developed and produced nickel sized hail. Lightning destroyed a large estate size home in Longwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning associated with a strong thunderstorm struck a large estate home in the Sweetwater Club subdivision of Longwood. The home became fully engulfed in flames and officials declared it a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was over the area aloft with the surface Bermuda ridge axis south of the area. A light and mean southwest steering flow was over the area with an unstable low level air mass in place. Storms formed along the sea breezes and propagated inland through the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trail of wind damage was reported from power lines down on Mayflower Street to power lines blown down on Talbot Avenue. Trees were also blown down near the Commonwealth and Edgewood intersection. Roof damage was reported on Edgewater Drive. The cost of damage was estimated. The Perez Ministry building on Edgewood Avenue near Kerle Street had its roof ripped off of the front and thunderstorm winds also knocked down two columns in front of the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported south of Kingsland. Nickel size hail was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported along Kelly Smith Ranch Road off of West River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a family of 3 that was boating on Thomas Creek on the north side of Jacksonville. One family member was taken to the hospital with life threatening injuries. The other two members were taken to the hospital as a precaution. The time and location were approximated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Lake Butler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge was centered west of the local area over the northern Gulf of Mexico. Precipitable water was 2.25 inches off of the 12Z JAX sounding. Storm initiated along the sea breezes and outflow boundaries during the afternoon when instability was very high with surface based CAPES of 3000-5000 J/kg. Scattered severe storms developed during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell through a roof at 1012 Northwest 51st Terrace. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon sea-breeze circulation and a weak upper level trough produced a few severe thunderstorms across the region with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near the intersection of State Highway 20 and County Road 287.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collision of the east coast and west coast sea breeze boundaries occurred to the west of Orlando during the late afternoon. A cluster of storms formed, producing frequent to excessive cloud to ground lightning. Lightning struck four homes within a 1 mile radius of Windermere, resulting in fires. Three of the house fires began within 2 hours of the lightning strikes. The fourth house fire erupted the following morning, after likely smoldering overnight. Two estates homes were destroyed and two smaller homes were damaged in the fires. Two minor indirect injuries resulted from fighting the fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent to excessive cloud to ground lightning erupted within a cluster of storms over western Orange County between 1530 and 1600 LST. During the next two hours, three homes in Windermere caught fire due to earlier lightning strikes. The first house, damaged by fire, was reported at 1644 LST. One fireman sustained minor indirect injuries due to smoke inhalation while fighting the fire. At 1652 LST, a fire was reported within a large estate home. The fire destroyed the home. A third house fire was reported nearby at 1740 LST and this home was damaged by fire. The following day, shortly before 0900 LST and 15 hours after the lightning storm, another estate home in Windermere caught fire and was destroyed. Fire officials believed that lightning from storms the previous afternoon struck the house and smoldered overnight. One fireman was treated for smoke inhalation while fighting this fire, an indirect, minor injury related to the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection moved inland from the Gulf of Mexico and produced an isolated downburst over the Apalachicola Municipal Airport.
East winds of 15 to 25 mph created a high risk for rip currents along the east coast beaches of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An officer on an ATV noticed two snorkelers caught in a rip current and in distress at the Clarke Avenue beach in the Town of Palm Beach. The officer, along with assistance from 2 lifeguards, was able to rescue both men.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of severe weather in South Florida produces high wind gusts, down trees, and lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glades County Sherriff���s office reported a tree down in Moore Haven along with widespread power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of severe weather in South Florida produces high wind gusts, down trees, and lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ramp-side worker at the Ft Lauderdale/Hollywood International Airport was injured when lightning struck nearby. The morning incident occurred at gate F6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm briefly became severe and produced damaging winds, affecting a small area in St. Cloud. Another strong storm west of Vero Beach produced an outflow boundary which intersected a newly developing cell, resulting in funnel cloud and brief tornado (landspout) touchdown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two thunderstorms merged over St. Cloud and the resultant storm produced a locally damaging microburst. The microburst winds uprooted four trees with diameters of 17 to 24 inches, north of Highway 192 and east of Narcoossee Road. A shed was also damaged on the property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado occurred 2 miles northeast of Belle Glade.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Palm Beach County Sherriff���s office reported a tornado 2 miles northeast of the Palm Beach County Sherriff's office substation along state road 80; however no damages or injuries occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable air mass resulted in the development of afternoon thunderstorms that produced some severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to a screen porch and shingles of a home occurred along with trees blown down.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf and dangerous rip currents affected the east central Florida coast for several days, beginning August 28. Rough, breaking waves of 4 to 6 feet caused the drowning of a surfer in Brevard County. Brevard County life guards also rescued 68 people from rough surf and rip currents on August 28 alone. Life guards in adjacent Volusia County reported several dozen ocean rescues. The rough surf and dangerous rip currents persisted for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man drowned off Seagull Park in Satellite Beach at 1350 LST August 28, while surfing within the high surf associated with distant Hurricane Danielle. Indications are that the surfer was hit on the head by his surf board as a result of successive 4 to 6 foot swells, and subsequently drowned. Life guards rescued nearly 100 people from the beaches of Brevard and Volusia Counties on August 28 alone. The high surf and dangerous rip currents persisted for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure was in place across the area with the ridge axis to the north providing a predominant light easterly wind. With daytime heating and the moist environment in place, scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms developed along sea breeze and outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree and caused it to fall on a house on Flora Road. There was significant damage to the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure in place at the surface and aloft kept a hot and moderately moist atmosphere over the area. Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed along sea breeze and outflow boundaries, with some storms producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an 84-year-old Cracker-style home in Lehigh Acres and consumed the entire structure. The historic home, which was uninhabited, was one of the first homes built in the city. The time is estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable southwest flow was established at the surface and aloft. In addition, a series of upper disturbances were moving over the area. When combined with daytime heating and sea breeze boundaries, this allowed for numerous strong to severe thunderstorms to develop through the day and continue into the evening, with damaging winds, hail, and a waterspout occurring with the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two mobile homes with carport damage, a mobile home had a secondary roof peeled off, and a tree fell onto the roof of another mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed for afternoon sea breeze circulations to develop each day. The warm and moist atmosphere caused scattered thunderstorms containing damaging winds to form along the sea breeze and outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported damage to a lanai roof and screen on a vacant home and a few tree branches were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout formed within a thunderstorm over the intracoastal river, then moved onshore the adjacent barrier island in New Smyrna Beach and produced minor damage as it crossed to the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a line of thunderstorms moved from the mainland to the Volusia County coast, a|waterspout formed over Browns Bay, a marshy area east of the intracoastal river. The waterspout moved onshore the adjacent barrier island in New Smyrna Beach and produced minor damage as it crossed to the Atlantic coast. Six homes received minor damage, and one home experienced more extensive damage as a small part of roofing plywood and shingles was removed. Insulation was blown to the ocean about a half mile away. See separate waterspout entry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Life guards rescued over 200 people along the Atlantic coast of South Florida during this late July week. The rip currents were created by persistent and strong east winds caused by high pressure north and east of the area. Wind gusts up to 44 MPH were noted during this event which peaked between July 20th and 22nd. These winds also caused rough seas on the local Atlantic waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life guards at Fort Lauderdale rescued 116 people over a three-day period, with an additional 8 at Hallandale Beach. Two of the rescued people at Fort Lauderdale were taken to Broward General Hospital. At an unguarded beach in Hallandale, two people were helped ashore, however one lady went under water for near 20 minutes and suffered from cardiac arrest. She did regain a pulse and was in critical condition at Aventura Medical Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Bonnie made landfall in Miami-Dade County during the afternoon of July 23rd with marginal tropical storm conditions along the coast of Biscayne Bay. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 knots were common across the southeast coast metro area along with rainfall totals of 2 to 3.25 inches in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Bonnie made landfall near Cutler Bay in southern Miami-Dade County during the late morning of July 23rd. Maximum sustained winds were near 40 mph as the storm made landfall. Minimum central pressure was 1008 mb. Effects over south Florida were minimal, with the only areas experiencing sustained tropical storm force winds right along the coast in Virginia Key south of downtown Miami. Highest wind gusts over most of Miami-Dade County were in the 40 to 45 mph range. ||Widely scattered power outages were the primary impact (about 14,000 customers), along with negligible damage to newly planted small to medium sized trees and downed power lines. ||Rainfall associated with the storm was also relatively minor, with maximum storm total amounts of 2.5 to 3.25 inches over central and northern Miami-Dade County. Minor urban flooding was the result. Highest storm surge was 0.92 feet at Virginia Key at 806 AM EDT. No significant coastal flooding or beach erosion was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm drifting southwest from Florida Bay produced frequent lightning at Big Pine Key.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tiki hut at the Big Pine Fishing Lodge on Big Pine Key.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure over the western Atlantic produced moderate to strong east winds across south Florida. This led to the development of strong rip currents along the east coast beaches. Three persons drowned as a result of the strong rip currents, and as many as several hundred people were rescued during a three-day period along the beaches of Palm Beach, Broward and Miami-Dade counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple visiting the area from Georgia drowned in rip currents in Lantana. A 57-year-old woman got into trouble after being pulled by a rip current. Her 70-year-old husband jumped in to save her, but was also caught in the current. Two Good Samaritans jumped in the water to try to save the couple without success.||The drownings occurred at an unguarded section of the beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent high pressure over the western Atlantic produced moderate to strong east winds across south Florida. This led to the development of strong rip currents along the east coast beaches. Three persons drowned as a result of the strong rip currents, and as many as several hundred people were rescued during a three-day period along the beaches of Palm Beach, Broward and Miami-Dade counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local teenager and his friend were caught in rip currents while swimming on Fort Lauderdale Beach. One of the boys was rescued by emergency personnel, but an 18-year-old male was unable to be rescued. His body was spotted two days later near the same spot on the beach.||The beach was unguarded at the time of the incident because it was after hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated early evening thunderstorm developed just south of Wekiva High School, where a football game was underway. Lightning struck a nearby light post, injuring two people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated early evening thunderstorm developed just south of Wekiva High School, where a football game was underway. School officials suspended the game and asked spectators to evacuate the stadium. As the stadium was being cleared, lightning struck one of four towering light posts and two people were injured (one person was leaning against a metal fence adjacent to the stands). Both individuals were transported to the hospital and were later released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture ahead of an unseasonable cold front over North Florida produced widespread showers and thunderstorms over South Florida on July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boater was struck by lightning just east of Lighthouse Point. When the lightning struck the boat the antenna broke and shards of fiberglass shot into the boater's arm. The man was brought into a marina at Pompano Beach then taken to North Broward Medical Center, But did not appear to have serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture ahead of an unseasonable cold front over North Florida produced widespread showers and thunderstorms over South Florida on July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A passing thunderstorm at the North Palm Beach General Airport caused branches of two inches in diameter to break off. Winds were estimated at 40 to 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northern extension of a tropical wave caused another day of showers and thunderstorms over South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hollywood police department reported flood waters entering multiple homes east of Federal Highway east of Sheridan Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure over Florida triggered a round of strong thunderstorms over South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The community Methodist Church in Lake Harbor was struck by lightning around mid day. The strike split the ceiling, noted by the wood that had fallen in the church aisles, and relocated the air conditioner cover to the front of the church. No water damage was noted from the thunderstorm, however phones and the air conditioner where out so Sunday services were relocated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continuation of deep southwest flow of tropical moisture along with a low pressure trough in the Gulf of Mexico provided for strong to severe afternoon showers and thunderstorms over parts of south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from a thunderstorm knocked down a big pine tree onto SW 61st Ave in Davie. Palm trees were also knocked down along University and Nova Drives. Pea size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure over Florida triggered a round of strong thunderstorms over South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a transformer behind The Colonnade on 5th. The strike blew the transformer box apart, sent a fireball down The Colonnade's breezeway, and cut power to several businesses for the day. Firefighters responded and had to pour foam on the burning transformer after FPL cut power to the unit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable southwest flow was established at the surface and aloft. In addition, a series of upper disturbances were moving over the area. When combined with daytime heating and sea breeze boundaries, this allowed for numerous strong to severe thunderstorms to develop through the day and continue into the evening, with damaging winds, hail, and a waterspout occurring with the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blowing west to east knocked down 15 power poles, two distribution lines, and one high-voltage transmission line along Highway 98 South, from near Clubhouse Road or County Road 540 to Winter Lake Road or State Road 540.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds caused rip currents to form over the Atlantic beaches of South Florida. Two people were rescued near the Lake Worth pier from rip currents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A teenager and his family were caught in a rip current near the Lake Worth pier. The teenager was able to escape the grip of the rip and help his family to shore when he noticed a couple of girls also caught in the strong current. He then called 911, but a pair of Good Samaritans on surf boards rescued to girls from a rip current. The two girls were taken to an area hospital, and were expected to make full recoveries.||No lifeguards were on duty because the incident occurred after-hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Life guards rescued over 200 people along the Atlantic coast of South Florida during this late July week. The rip currents were created by persistent and strong east winds caused by high pressure north and east of the area. Wind gusts up to 44 MPH were noted during this event which peaked between July 20th and 22nd. These winds also caused rough seas on the local Atlantic waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miami Beach Ocean Rescue saved the lives of 66 people during the period of July 20 through the 22nd. No serious injuries were reported. 2 children were also rescued at Haulover Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Life guards rescued over 200 people along the Atlantic coast of South Florida during this late July week. The rip currents were created by persistent and strong east winds caused by high pressure north and east of the area. Wind gusts up to 44 MPH were noted during this event which peaked between July 20th and 22nd. These winds also caused rough seas on the local Atlantic waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life guards at Palm Beach County beaches rescued 26 people from rip currents from the 20th through the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms trained northward near the Florida east-central coast, within a strong southerly flow due in part to the presence of Tropical Depression Five remnants near the central Gulf coast. Around mid day, a waterspout developed over the intracoastal river and moved onshore the mainland in Edgewater, producing minor damage. Several hours later, another storm developed rotation and produced a brief touchdown in a wooded area, farther inland over southern Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout developed over the intracoastal waterway and moved onshore and inland for a short distance before dissipating as the circulation approached US Highway 1. The waterspout developed in association with an isolated, heavy shower that peaked in intensity near the time of the touchdown. The waterspout moved onshore the mainland as a very weak tornado and first impacted Cameron's Marina, where a small boat on a trailer was overturned, damaging an adjacent pickup truck. The weak tornado then continued moving inland on a northwest track, causing soffit damage to another residence and minor tree damage along the remainder of the path. The waterspout/tornado was observed and photographed by many individuals. Interestingly, a condensation funnel was not visible throughout its short life cycle, however, swirling water and debris were apparent and were lifted far aloft. See separate event for waterspout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms trained northward near the Florida east-central coast, within a strong southerly flow due in part to the presence of Tropical Depression Five remnants near the central Gulf coast. Around mid day, a waterspout developed over the intracoastal river and moved onshore the mainland in Edgewater, producing minor damage. Several hours later, another storm developed rotation and produced a brief touchdown in a wooded area, farther inland over southern Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volusia County EVAC Ambulance personnel reported a tornado touchdown in a heavily wooded area just west of Interstate 95 and south of State Road 44 in rural Volusia County to the west of New Smyrna Beach. The brief tornado touchdown occurred along the southwest edge of a cluster of strong thunderstorms which were training slowly northward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed in association with an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 year old man was struck and killed by lightning while vacationing on Saint Joe Beach just prior to 1:30 pm EDT as reported by local newspapers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening hours in association with the sea-breeze boundary and an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening hours in association with the sea-breeze boundary and an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was an isolated, short-lived microburst that developed quickly and dissipated quickly over the airport.  No damage was reported anywhere in Tallahassee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents developed and resulted in one critical injury and several other rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One man was transported to the hospital in critical condition after being rescued near Ocean Towers on Panama City Beach.  Several other rescues also occurred on this day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents developed and resulted in one fatality and two injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three men became trapped in a rip current off St. Joseph State Park.  All three were pulled from the water.  Two were transported to area hospitals and one of those was unable to be revived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The collision of the sea-breeze boundary with various outflow boundaries resulted in a slow moving thunderstorms over northern parts of Tallahassee with flash flooding occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tallahassee Democrat newspaper reported that flash flooding from a strong thunderstorm occurred along the Shamrock Drive roundabout in Killearn Estates.  One woman had gone through the intersection 15 minutes prior to the flash flood without any problems, but when she returned, the water stalled her car and she had to temporarily abandon it.  Some roads were impassable, and water 2 feet deep stalled two cars.  Another motorist reported that visibility was barely 10 feet, and by the time they hit the water it was too late with water rushing through the car.  Two homes were also flooded along the Timber Lake neighborhood.  A rain gauge in the Turkey Roost Subdivision off of Baum Road north of I-10 recorded 5.00 of rain.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index was 113 degrees between 4 pm EDT and 5 pm EDT at the Apalachicola airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Apalachicola airport reported heat index values frequently exceeding 110 degrees from 12 pm EDT through 6 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values at the Marianna airport occasionally rose to 110 degrees between 1 pm EDT and 5 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index at the Marianna airport rose to 110 degrees or higher from 2 pm EDT to 5 pm EDT.  The peak value was 113 degrees at 3 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index exceeded 110 degrees from 12 pm EDT to 2 pm EDT and peaked at 112 degrees from 12 pm EDT to 1 pm EDT at the Perry airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed in association with an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wakulla News newspaper reported that a house was hit by lightning and destroyed on Shoemaker Court off of Ivan Church Road in Crawfordville.  Nobody was home at the time.  Two dogs were rescued.  The damage was listed at $272,000 according to the newspaper.  A jogger also reportedly suffered an electric shock when lightning struck the ground nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index peaked at 110 degrees at the Cross City airport at 3 pm EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index rose to 110 degrees at 6 pm EDT at the new Panama City airport (KECP).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Breaking surf reached 5 to 9 feet due to long period swells of 15 to 17 seconds associated with Hurricane Igor passing well offshore and parallel to the Florida east coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent surface trough was in place to the north of the area with an upper trough in place over the area. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed each day in the very moist and unstable environment. Long durations of heavy rainfall accumulations caused some flooding and strong gusty winds with storms caused some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two eastbound lanes of State Road 70 were closed near the intersection of Lockwood Ridge Road due to street flooding from heavy rains.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed each day in the very moist and unstable environment. Long durations of heavy rainfall accumulations caused some flooding and strong gusty winds with storms caused some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 40W was closed near the intersection of 51st Street due to street flooding from heavy rains.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed each day in the very moist and unstable environment. Long durations of heavy rainfall accumulations caused some flooding and strong gusty winds with storms caused some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was covering State Road 24 near Rosewood due to heavy rains.

Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed each day in the very moist and unstable environment. Long durations of heavy rainfall accumulations caused some flooding and strong gusty winds with storms caused some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was damage to two lanais on southeast 40th Terrace.
