Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed each day in the very moist and unstable environment. Long durations of heavy rainfall accumulations caused some flooding and strong gusty winds with storms caused some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several streets that were closed due to street flooding from heavy rains in the Orange Blossom Estates area southwest of Sebring. Based off of radar, an estimated three to five inches of rain fell across that area. Portions of Lily Street, Maxanna Boulevard and Apple Road had closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter observed a funnel cloud for approximately 10 minutes, a few miles south of State Road 60.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter observed formation of a funnel cloud a few miles south of his location along highway 60. The funnel persisted about 10 minutes and was photographed. Weather radar did not indicate any showers or storms in the area. The fair weather funnel cloud likely formed along a small-scale cloud boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast developing storm just north of Windermere moved slowly south and produced frequent to excessive cloud to ground lightning. Lightning struck a home and the subsequent fire destroyed the home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a strong storm over Windermere struck a home and caused a fire which destroyed the home. Two firemen were treated for smoke inhalation while fighting the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 2 and a half year old was left in a van and died due to hyperthermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2 year old girl was found in a van at a day care at around 4 PM local time after spending many hours in the vehicle. The child had been picked up in the morning and was found in the vehicle about 6 hours later.||The high temperature in the area was 91 degrees, which is very close to the average  high for early August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid/upper level low pressure over Central Florida helped to produce afternoon thunderstorms over much of south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A teen was fishing at a lake near Lake Worth Road and the Turnpike in the Vista Pines Community when he was struck by lightning. A man witnessed the teen being struck and rushed to help. When arriving at the scene the teen was not breathing, bleeding out the nose, and burned, but he was revived and then rushed to Delray Medical Center where he was able to move his extremities and was later able to talk.||The boy was expected to recover from his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture and strong easterly winds in place across the area allowed for numerous thunderstorms to develop with afternoon heating. The storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a viewer report of powerlines and powerpoles downed near the intersection of Wheeler Street and Baker Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and moist airmass in place across the area allowed for heat indices to reach 100 to 105 degrees and an afternoon seabreeze to develop each day. Scattered thunderstorms developed each afternoon along the boundary and a girl was injured by lightning in one storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15-year-old girl was inside a residence on Easy Street in Lecanto, using a hair appliance, when she was injured by a lightning strike. She was transported to the hospital in stable condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and moist airmass in place across the area allowed for heat indices to reach 100 to 105 degrees and an afternoon seabreeze to develop each day. Scattered thunderstorms developed each afternoon along the boundary and a girl was injured by lightning in one storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 3-year-old girl was mistakenly left in a sport utility vehicle for around an hour and 45 minutes, and died in the heat, while three of the girl's cousins attended a church service on North 9th Street in Tampa. Temperatures during the time the girl was in the vehicle were in the upper 80s, with dew point temperatures around 75 degrees, making heat index values around 99 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable atmospheric conditions led to thunderstorm development along the sea breeze front during the afternoon hours, leading to convergence of strong storms over southern Palm Beach which triggered a tornado in the Boca Raton area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small and short-lived tornado moved through the West Boca area, with numerous reports received of trees down, overturned patio furniture, street lights knocked down, some roofing shingles blown off houses, and downed power lines from around the intersection of Powerline Road and SW 18th Street to the Boca Point Golf Course. No major structural damage was reported.||No damage assessment was performed by county officials due to the minor nature of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled and dissipating frontal boundary was in place to the north of the area with high pressure ridging across south Florida. Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in the moist west to southwesterly flow each afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home and started a fire that consumed the 5,000 square foot structure and it's contents. The family was out of town at the time, so there were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and slow-moving thunderstorms produced flooding in the Jupiter area due to light atmospheric flow and copious moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported severe street flooding in Jupiter with Central Blvd and Indian Creek Parkway closed. Rainfall reports from the area were of 3.75 inches of rain in only 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm developed during the afternoon hours in association with an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down in central Jefferson county.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed in association with an unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from an afternoon thunderstorm led to brief, minor street flooding in Madison with no damage or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index hovered near 110 degrees between 2 pm EDT and 4 pm EDT and peaked at 113 degrees at 4 pm EDT at the Cross City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled and dissipating frontal boundary was in place to the north of the area with high pressure ridging across south Florida. Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in the moist west to southwesterly flow each afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a barbed-wire fence in a cow pasture and injured a 9-year-old boy who was reaching through the fence to pet a cow. The boy briefly lost consciousness and couldn't move his right side after the strike. He was hospitalized overnight and regained feeling in his right side. The strike also knocked down an 11-year-old boy, a cow, and a calf that were all near the fence, but all stood back up within seconds uninjured. The strike was the first strike in the area, with the thunderstorm moving in shortly thereafter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dewpoint values in the 80s combined with temperatures in the 90s produced a long stretch of heat index values frequently above 110 degrees from 10 am EDT to 8 pm EDT at the Apalachicola airport.  The heat index exceeded 115 degrees from 1 pm EDT until 3 pm EDT with a peak value of 124 degrees at 2:38 pm EDT.  The temperature at that time was 95 degrees and the dewpoint was 84.2 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dewpoint values in the 80s combined with temperatures in the 90s to produce heat index values occasionally surpassing 115 degrees from 12 pm EDT through 4 pm EDT at the Cross City airport.  The heat index peaked at 117 degrees at 3 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled and dissipating frontal boundary was in place to the north of the area with high pressure ridging across south Florida. Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in the moist west to southwesterly flow each afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An airlines employee was injured by lightning that struck the outside tarmac or ramp area near where they were standing at Tampa International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index occasionally exceeded 110 degrees from 3 pm EDT to 6 pm EDT at the new Panama City airport (KECP).  The peak value was 114 degrees at 6 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index occasionally exceeded 110 degrees from 4 pm EDT to 7 pm EDT at the Tallahassee airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and high humidity combined to produce heat index values above 110 degrees across parts of the Florida panhandle at the end of the month.  Heat index values even exceeded 115 degrees in a few locations on occasion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index value frequently exceeded 110 degrees from 2 pm EDT to 4 pm EDT and peaked at 113 degrees at 4 pm EDT at the Perry airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed with lightning striking a home off Indiangrass Lane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home on Indiangrass Lane, which resulted in a fire. Firefighters were able to keep the damage to a minimum.  One firefighter received minor injuries while battling the blaze.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 6 month old child was left in a car for approximately 2 hours as afternoon temperatures reached the lower 90s in the Tallahassee area.  The child eventually succumbed to injuries believed to be related to prolonged heat exposure.
Heavy rain fell across Madison County as a low pressure system moved through the southeast U.S.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Madison County Carrier newspaper reported that a rain gauge on East Bunker Street registered 4.25 inches of rain from midnight through 11:25 am EDT.  Water covered parts of Osgood Street behind Clover Farm, and one vehicle stalled in the water.  County work crews cleared the access path to the holding pond to help clear water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Typical summer humidity and instability led to numerous evening thunderstorms and lightning across southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house during Labor Day festivities in Southwest Ranches. Tiles were blown from the roof and a fire was started on the roof as well. The fire was extinguished and no other damages were known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-latitude trough extending from New England to central Florida led to very unstable conditions across south Florida. This resulted in the development of numerous afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Pembroke Pines blowing a small hole in the roof and roofing tiles onto a parked car. The tiles caused minor damage to the car. No fire was started by the strike, however power was knocked out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: East to northeast winds of 15 to 20 knots along the southeast Florida coast led to a high risk of rip currents along all Atlantic beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 16 year old male was pulled from the water near the Lake Worth pier. Fire rescue officials said the boy was foaming at the mouth when crews arrived, but was conscious by the time he arrived at JFK Medical Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents persisted across the east-central Florida waters in the aftermath of Hurricane Earl. Rough surf on September 1 and 2 kept most swimmers out of the ocean, but once swell heights lessened and more swimmers entered the water, life guards rescued several hundred people who became caught in rip currents. One drowning and one serious injury resulted from the rip currents in Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong rip currents were observed along area beaches due to persistent, although diminishing long-period swells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions (D2) developed across Jackson and Holmes counties on September 14th.  This drought continued into October.
Severe drought conditions (D2) developed across Jackson and Holmes counties on September 14th.  This drought continued into October.
Onshore winds of 15 to 20 mph led to strong rip currents along the Palm Beach County beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15 year old girl and her boyfriend were pulled away from shore by a rip current, however the 16 year old male managed to pull the female back to shore. The girl's 38 year old stepfather was also at the beach in Palm Beach when he was also pulled out 50 yards from shore. A officer and lifeguard pulled the man back to shore. The girl and stepfather were taken to a local hospital and were both later released in good condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southeast flow across south Florida led to the development of afternoon and evening thunderstorms over the interior and western parts of the peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house and started a fire near the main entry on Saddle Brook Lane in North Naples. North Naples Fire Rescue responded and had the fire extinguished with in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture associated with a tropical disturbance (eventual TD 16) south of Cuba produced scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across all of south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a man at Frenchman's Bend in Palm Beach Gardens. He was taken to the front office by some landscapers where he was then taken to Saint Mary's Medical Center by emergency crews. He was in stable condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions across much of northeast Florida helped a wildfire grow in the Ocala National Forest, south of Juniper Springs, for several days. On October 16th, the wildfire was around 5 acres big. By the 19th, the fire had grown to approximately 1000 acres On the 23rd, the wildfire was 1826 acres, and was named the Bombing Range Nine Wildfire. On the 24th, the forest service reported that the fire had grown to 2136 acres. On the 27th, the fire was 2436 acres per the U.S. Forest Service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Forest Service reported a wildfire in the Ocala National Forest south of Juniper Springs on Oct. 16th that was about 5 acres in size. Dry conditions continued to help the file grow and by Oct. 27th, the fire had grown to 2436 acres. On Oct. 30th, the fire had grown to 3111 acres and was named Bombing Range Nine. It should be noted that October 2010 was the driest October on record for Jacksonville Florida and Gainesville Florida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few afternoon thunderstorms developed with one storm becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down with winds estimated at 50 knots at the FSU Reservation near the intersection of Lake Bradford and Flastacowo roads.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the deep south while the mid and upper level low moved over the Mississippi River Valley.  A surface low developed over south Georgia in the afternoon, and a trailing squall line moved across the area in the afternoon and evening. Strong low level shear, including a 50 to 60 knot low level jet, combined with adequate instability to produce widespread severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down around 1322 pm EST in Bradford county. The path width varied from 200 to 400 yards, and the path length was around 9 to possibly 10 miles. Numerous outbuildings were damaged and destroyed. There was mostly minor damage to substantial structures. At least a dozen homes were damaged along County Road 229.  There were 25 homes that suffered minor damage and 28 that had major damage. A total of 23 houses were destroyed. The emergency manager estimated $2.5 to $3 million dollars in damages to homes and about $500,000 in damages to out buildings. The tornado traveled just north of due east and lifted in western Clay County were the path may have gone over the Camp Blanding National Guard Auxiliary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the deep south while the mid and upper level low moved over the Mississippi River Valley.  A surface low developed over south Georgia in the afternoon, and a trailing squall line moved across the area in the afternoon and evening. Strong low level shear, including a 50 to 60 knot low level jet, combined with adequate instability to produce widespread severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down around 1322 pm EST in Bradford County and continued for a brief period as an EF0 event in Clay County. The path width varied from 200 to 400 yards, and the total path length was around 10 miles. The storm was only present in a small portion of Clay County and entered the Camp Blanding National Guard Facility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning storm produced localized severe thunderstorm wind damage in Orange County. ||A cell exhibiting rotation on radar tracked from rural western Brevard county, producing isolated severe thunderstorm wind damage in Viera from several miles west of I-95 to I-95 where a tractor trailer was tipped over. Just east of I-95, an EF0 tornado touched down and caused minor structural damage to several hundred homes, as well as considerable tree damage along a continuous path over 7 miles. Along this path, the tornado moved from the mainland, across the Indian River, Merritt Island, Banana River and finally across the beach side barrier island before moving into the Atlantic. See separate waterspout episode for over-water portion of tornadic waterspout track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down just east of Interstate 95 in the town of Viera in central Brevard County and remained on the ground for over 7 miles, then exited into the Atlantic. The tornado moved from the mainland, across the Indian River, to Merritt Island, then across the Banana River to the beach-side barrier island between Patrick Air Force Base and Cocoa Beach, then exited into the Atlantic. One hundred and forty three homes sustained minor damage in Viera, mainly damaged/destroyed screen pool enclosures, damage to soffits, gutters and some shingles. Minor damage to six homes occurred on Merritt Island and four apartment units and several vehicles in Cocoa Beach. Numerous trees were toppled or snapped along the entire swath of damage. One person was slightly injured in Viera due to flying debris/glass. EF assessment: DI2, DOD2, LB-EXP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents persisted across the east-central Florida waters in the aftermath of Hurricane Earl. Rough surf on September 1 and 2 kept most swimmers out of the ocean, but once swell heights lessened and more swimmers entered the water, life guards rescued several hundred people who became caught in rip currents. One drowning and one serious injury resulted from the rip currents in Volusia County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over two hundred swimmers were rescued by Volusia County Beach Patrol on September 4 alone. Around 1000 LST, two teenagers were caught in a rip current and rescued by bystanders. One of the teenagers was transported to a local hospital were she later died. Around 2000 LST, a 19 year old was found face down in the ocean after being trapped in a rip current. The victim was transported to a local hospital, where he remained in a coma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe drought conditions (D2) which developed in mid September in Holmes and Jackson counties worsened.  Extreme drought conditions (D3) developed on October 19th and continued into November.
The severe drought conditions (D2) which developed in mid September in Holmes and Jackson counties worsened.  Extreme drought conditions (D3) developed on October 19th and continued into November.

Severe drought conditions (D2) developed in Washington and northern Walton counties on October 5th. Extreme drought conditions (D3) developed in Washington and northern Walton counties on October 19th and continued into November.

High surf associated with distant Hurricane Earl reached 5 to 9 feet along the east central Florida coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Breaking surf reached 5 to 9 feet due to long period swells of 12 to 15 seconds associated with Hurricane Earl passing well offshore and parallel to the Florida east coast.

Afternoon seabreeze thunderstorms developed each day, with later timing on the 14th. Lightning from these scattered thunderstorms struck on individual and caused damage to trees. ||Moderate onshore flow caused strong rip currents near Upham Beach. A 16-year-old boy became caught in the current and was pulled out to sea. A 16-year-old girl then swam out to rescue him and also became caught in the current. The two were finally rescued by two surfers who reached them on their surf boards, but the foursome was then swept 150 yards offshore and were struggling to get back to the beach. Two girls on a jet ski then reached them and towed the four back to shore. The two swimmers were hospitalized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and knocked down two trees near the intersection of East 32nd Avenue and North 57th Street in Tampa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across the area in the warm and moist environment behind Tropical Storm Bonnie. An individual was struck by lightning from one of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59-year-old woman was struck by lightning at Fred Howard Park and transported to the hospital with injuries. She suffered burns to her hip and injured her nose when she fell face down after the strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the region allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon along seabreeze boundaries. Lightning from the storms caused damage to a few homes and struck an individual.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house near the intersection of 19th Avenue North and 60th Street North and made a hole in the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the region allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon along seabreeze boundaries. Lightning from the storms caused damage to a few homes and struck an individual.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a large house and caused a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the region allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon along seabreeze boundaries. Lightning from the storms caused damage to a few homes and struck an individual.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 45-year-old construction worker was struck by lightning while packing up his tools on a jobsite in North Port. The strike entered his right knee and exited his left foot. He suffered burns around the entrance and exit areas and was hospitalized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was in place across the region, limiting moisture, but allowing for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms along seabreeze boundaries. Some thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds estimated at 50 MPH at a RV dealership damaged 26 RV trailers and totaled 16 RV trailers. The trailers and campers were not tied down or secured, and most of the damage was from one trailer tipping over onto another and trailers getting pushed into each other. There was no other damage at the dealership to trees, powerlines, or fencing, and the damage path was all in one direction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning thunderstorms rolled through South Florida in strong easterly flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miami-Dade Fire Rescue responded to a man struck by lightning at NE 163 Street and 10th Avenue in North Miami Beach. The victim was transported to Jackson North Hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging south of the area lifted north to bring prevailing westerly winds on the 12th and 13th around to the southeast on the 14th. Afternoon seabreeze thunderstorms developed each day, with later timing on the 14th. Lightning from these scattered thunderstorms struck on individual and caused damage to trees. ||Moderate onshore flow caused strong rip currents near Upham Beach. A 16-year-old boy became caught in the current and was pulled out to sea. A 16-year-old girl then swam out to rescue him and also became caught in the current. The two were finally rescued by two surfers who reached them on their surf boards, but the foursome was then swept 150 yards offshore and were struggling to get back to the beach. Two girls on a jet ski then reached them and towed the four back to shore. The two swimmers were hospitalized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 65-year-old male was struck by lightning while outside at the Feather Sound Country Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southerly wind flow along with upper level disturbances moving across Florida led to an active day of thunderstorms across south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 19-year-old woman was shocked by a lightning strike which struck about 25 feet from her home's driveway as she was outside loading clothes into a vehicle. The woman was 8 months pregnant. The victim was taken to a nearby hospital and was released the same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe wind gusts, between 58 and 69 mph, occurred during a 10-minute period across the northeast portion of the Kennedy Space Center as strong storms moving seaward collided with the sea breeze boundary and intensified to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 64 mph were recorded by a USAF wind tower at the Kennedy Space Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf associated with distant Hurricane Earl reached 5 to 9 feet along the east central Florida coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Breaking surf reached 5 to 9 feet due to long period swells of 12 to 15 seconds associated with Hurricane Earl passing well offshore and parallel to the Florida east coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level short wave trough traversed the area during the afternoon and evening which enhanced instability over the area. Sea breeze and outflow induced storms were slow moving to the east with surface based CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Aloft at 500 mb temperatures were -8 to -10 degrees Celcius.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a structure fire in Northern Palm Coast. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging across the area allowed for afternoon sea breeze circulations to develop each day. The warm and moist atmosphere caused scattered thunderstorms containing damaging winds to form along the sea breeze and outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor roof damage occurred to the Kings Way Christian Center in North Fort Myers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the area and combined with a strong low-level jet and a strong upper-level jet in place over the state. The warm and moist environment in place allowed for numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop. A few tornadoes touched down and some areas received heavy rains causing flooding from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree sustained damage near Baypoint.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell developed over the Everglades and moved into the Broward County Metropolitan Area where it spawned a tornado in Oakland Park. Heavy rainfall also occurred as a squall line moved through the metro area after the tornado event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell became organized as it moved out of the Everglades and into the Broward County Metro area. A tornado warning was issued at 754 AM, for areas from Weston to North Lauderdale along the storm���s path, and at 827 AM the storm produced a brief EF-0 tornado.  It first touched down in the Royal Palm Isles section of Oakland Park just west of I-95, and traveled east northeast for 2 miles before lifting near the intersection of NE 11th Ave and Floranada.  The tornado was approximately 100 yards wide and damage was mostly confined to trailers, cars, trees, and some roofs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough in the mid levels traverse the area in the afternoon over an unstable and moist low level air mass. This energy combined with convergence along the sea breezes and resultant outflow boundaries created scattered severe storms in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An RV Park employee reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front was over north Florida as a mid and upper level ridge center was south of the region. A short wave trough traversed the area in the afternoon and combined with the sea breezes and outflows over an unstable air mass scattered severe storms developed across northeast Florida in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Naval Air Station Jacksonville measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The collision of the East Coast and Gulf Coast sea breeze boundaries over the central Florida interior resulted in several storms which produced damaging winds and penny sized hail from Oviedo to Orlando to St. Cloud. Trees fell onto two mobile homes and produced minor damage. A young boy in one of the mobile homes sustained minor injuries to his arm as a tree crashed onto the home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was observed during a thunderstorm, near the intersection of Dean Road and University Boulevard by a citizen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over the area with a surface trough located north of the Altamaha River basin. This energy combined with a moist and unstable air mass enabled scattered severe storms to form over the area along and in advance of the sea breezes and resultant outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two brief funnel clouds were spotted near the St. Johns River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active upper level disturbance moving across Florida triggered severe afternoon thunderstorms over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported quarter-sized hail on the ground near NW 36th Street and 41st Avenue in south Hialeah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure ridging south of the area lifted north to bring prevailing westerly winds on the 12th and 13th around to the southeast on the 14th. Afternoon seabreeze thunderstorms developed each day, with later timing on the 14th. Lightning from these scattered thunderstorms struck on individual and caused damage to trees. ||Moderate onshore flow caused strong rip currents near Upham Beach. A 16-year-old boy became caught in the current and was pulled out to sea. A 16-year-old girl then swam out to rescue him and also became caught in the current. The two were finally rescued by two surfers who reached them on their surf boards, but the foursome was then swept 150 yards offshore and were struggling to get back to the beach. Two girls on a jet ski then reached them and towed the four back to shore. The two swimmers were hospitalized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor damage occurred when approximately 50 MPH winds moved through areas of Zephyrhills. A few small trees and limbs were downed on Wire Road, Coolwood Road, and Kossik Road, there was minor damage to a mobile home roof on Ash Street, and a shed was damaged on Kossik Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from late night thunderstorms sparked two house fires in Dixie County, destroying both homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dixie County Times newspaper reported that lightning struck a single wide mobile home, which resulted in a fire that destroyed the home, van, boat, motor home, and two sheds.  Two cats were also killed in the fire.  The monetary damage figure provided is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused flooding in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains flooded the road on Webster Drive at Northview Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms focused along a convergence zone indirectly related to Tropical Storm Nicole produced heavy rainfall and extensive street flooding in Islamorada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused extensive street flooding over Lower and Upper Matecumbe Keys within the Village of Islamorada.  Water entered one home on Lower Matecumbe Key, causing one family to be temporarily displaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across the deep south while the mid and upper level low moved over the Mississippi River Valley.  A surface low developed over south Georgia in the afternoon, and a trailing squall line moved across the area in the afternoon and evening. Strong low level shear, including a 50 to 60 knot low level jet, combined with adequate instability to produce widespread severe storms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of rainfall fell in 3 hours. Minor street flooding was observed.  The emergency manager relayed storm total rainfall reports of 4 to 5 inches across west and northeast Palm Coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line ahead of a cold front moved rapidly east through South Florida during the late afternoon and early evening of February 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at Coral Reef Senior High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong thunderstorms developed ahead of a strong cold front over portions of south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at Okeechobee Blvd and Congress Avenue in West Palm Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms along the Gulf coast dumped heavy amounts of rain with some flooding reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches with localized amounts of 11 to 14 inches.  This heavy rainfall resulted in prolonged and widespread flooding of roads and low-lying areas areas generally south of U.S. Highway 19 between Horseshoe Beach and Suwannee.  This is a rural area with no known property damage.
